research.dimension: Email pattern generation & ranking (candidate permutation + prior-probability ordering from name + domain)

key_findings.claim: Use a two-tier ranking: a domain-learned pattern (when known) always outranks generic priors. Only fall back to global priors when the domain pattern is unknown.
key_findings.detail: The single biggest accuracy lever is NOT the generic permutation order but whether you already know the target domain's house pattern. The audit KB proves companies are internally consistent: amadeus.com 203/209 first.last, purplle.com dominant first.l, opengov.com/visa.com single_token, trimble.com first_last, veeam.com f.last. Hunter/MailMeteor/Clearbit all do the same thing — they learn the org pattern from any confirmed address, then apply it to new names, and only guess from global priors otherwise. Concrete algorithm: (1) look up domain in KB (built from this audit's domain_kb.json + any addresses you later verify); if a dominant_shape exists with >=60% share, generate ONLY that shape (plus at most 1-2 fallbacks) and score it high (~0.9); (2) if domain unknown, emit the full generic-ordered candidate list scored by global priors. Note visa.com/opengov.com use single_token but the token construction differs (flast vs first vs last) so store the exact template string, not just the shape family.

key_findings.claim: Concrete ordered default candidate list (domain unknown), tuned to this India-heavy corporate audit: first.last is dominant and dot is the near-universal separator.
key_findings.detail: Recommended generation order with starting prior weights (given={first,last,domain}): 1) first.last (0.60) 2) flast i.e. f+last (0.12) 3) first (0.08) 4) firstlast (0.05) 5) first.l (0.04) 6) firstl (0.03) 7) f.last (0.02) 8) last.first (0.015) 9) last (0.01) 10) lastf (0.01) 11) first_last (0.01) 12) first-last (0.005) 13) lastfirst (0.005) 14) last.f (0.005). Rationale: the audit's corporate distribution is first.last 76%, single_token 14%, first.l 6.6%, first_last ~1%, f.last ~1%, first-last ~0.1%; separator is dot 2535 / none 442 / underscore 27 / hyphen 2. This is MUCH more dot-and-first.last-skewed than the generic Interseller 5M-company study (which for 10001+ employees gives first.last 56%, flast 22%, first 6.6%, first_last 3.6%). Your audience skews large-enterprise + Indian corporates, so weight first.last higher (~0.60) and demote underscore/hyphen to near-zero. For SMB/startup domains (few employees), boost bare-first: the generic data shows first-only jumps to 40-70% under 50 employees. If you have any headcount signal from LinkedIn, use a size-conditioned prior table (small=first-first, large=first.last-first).

key_findings.claim: Token-building rules must run BEFORE permutation: normalize, transliterate to ASCII, then expand nicknames — each producing name variants that multiply into the pattern templates.
key_findings.detail: Pipeline: (a) Unicode normalize NFKD + strip diacritics/transliterate (José->jose, Müller->muller, Nguyễn->nguyen) using a unidecode-style map; keep BOTH the transliterated form and, for non-Latin scripts where transliteration is ambiguous (Cyrillic/Arabic/Chinese), consider top-2 romanizations as extra candidates. (b) lowercase, strip punctuation/apostrophes (O'Brien->obrien), strip titles/suffixes (Dr, Jr, III). (c) Nickname expansion via a bidirectional diminutive table (Bob<->Robert, Bill<->William, Bob/Rob/Bobby, Dick->Richard, Peggy->Margaret, Kate/Katie->Katherine, Chris->Christopher/Christ­ine, Alex->Alexander/Alexandra, etc.) — generate candidates for BOTH the given (nickname) and formal names because companies use either. Order: try the name as-given first (people often use their real display name), then formal, then other diminutives. (d) Each name-variant crosses with each template, so dedupe the final set. Keep the raw display-name token too (some orgs use the exact profile handle).

key_findings.claim: Middle names, hyphenated and multi-word surnames need explicit expansion rules — they are a top source of wrong guesses.
key_findings.detail: Multi-word / compound SURNAME (e.g., 'Van Der Berg', 'De Souza', 'García Márquez'): generate variants = (i) join all parts no space (vanderberg), (ii) last token only (berg/marquez), (iii) first surname token only (garcia), (iv) hyphen/dot joined. HYPHENATED surname (Smith-Jones): variants = smithjones, smith-jones, smith, jones. MIDDLE name: usually dropped, but generate (i) drop middle (first.last — default), (ii) first.middle.last, (iii) firstm+last (f + middleinitial + last), (iv) first.m.last, (v) some orgs put middle initial: firstmlast. Weight drop-middle highest. In the audit, note KB locals like 'saravanan.gm', 'ashwath.s', 'bhargav.k', 'apeksha.ss' — these are first + (initials of remaining name tokens), a very common Indian pattern where the 'surname' is actually collapsed initials.

key_findings.claim: Indian naming conventions require dedicated handling: initial-based surnames, single-name individuals, and first+initials collapses are common and visible in the audit data.
key_findings.detail: India-specific rules (audit is India-heavy — treat as first-class): (1) Many South Indian names are FirstName + Initial(s) where the initial expands to father's name/village (e.g., 'Ashwath S', 'Bhargav K', 'Saravanan GM'). LinkedIn often shows the initial, not the expansion — so generate first.<initial>, first<initial>, and first-only, since the org may store either the initial or nothing. KB shows exactly this: harman.com 'saravanan.gm', amadeus.com 'ashwath.s','bhargav.k','apeksha.ss','abhishek.kd'. (2) SINGLE-NAME individuals (no surname at all — common in India, e.g. mononym 'Riya', or LinkedIn profile with one name): candidates = first, first+random-digits is NOT worth guessing, but do try first, and if a middle/second token exists treat it as surname. KB 'somewhere.com' shows bare first names (grace, roger, jossie). (3) When only initials are available for the surname, DO NOT fabricate a full last name. (4) Purplle (Indian, purplle.com) is dominantly first.l (abhishek.k, anjali.t) — Indian startups frequently use first.initial to avoid collisions, so bump first.l up the list for .in / Indian-company domains specifically. (5) Order for Indian corporates: first.last, first.l, first<l>, first, flast.

key_findings.claim: How the commercial tools rank, and the scoring model to copy: pattern-prior for generation, source-evidence + SMTP for the final confidence score.
key_findings.detail: Hunter: returns a 0-100 confidence 'score'; ~95 means the exact address was found in public sources (URLs returned with extracted_on/last_seen_on), ~60 means it was pattern-guessed from the domain's known format with no direct source; for accept-all domains the score is the estimated validity probability. MailMeteor: generates ~34 permutations, tests the most-common patterns for the company, SMTP-verifies each in real time, and labels Valid/Risky/Not Found (claims 97%+ on standardized-pattern companies). Clearbit/RocketReach similarly combine a crawled/contributed dataset (direct evidence) with pattern inference. Recommended scoring for your build: final_score = w1*pattern_prior + w2*domain_pattern_match + w3*source_evidence + w4*smtp_signal, where source_evidence (address seen in a real source) dominates, domain_pattern_match is strong, and smtp_signal is gated by the audit's provider rule — SMTP RCPT is decisive ONLY on Google Workspace/Proofpoint/Mimecast/Cisco IronPort (honest 550 5.1.1), and MUST be ignored as a negative on Microsoft 365 (550 5.4.1 policy blocks fire on valid mailboxes). So on M365 domains, rank by pattern prior + any source evidence and mark SMTP-unverifiable rather than downranking.

key_findings.claim: Never spray the full permutation set via SMTP — generate all, but probe in ranked order and stop on first accept; cap the emitted set.
key_findings.detail: MailMeteor and every reputable permutator warn that sending to all generated combos harms sender reputation and burns the domain's RCPT rate limits. Implementation: generate the full deduped candidate list (typically 15-40 addresses after name-variant expansion), sort by prior, then verify sequentially/lazily and short-circuit on the first Valid. On providers where RCPT is unreliable (M365), skip probing and return the top pattern-prior candidate with an 'unverifiable / policy-blocked provider' flag. Batch/throttle RCPT probes per MX host and reuse one SMTP session. Also filter out role/catch-all locals (careers, hr, engineering, info — present in KB e.g. hackerearth.com 'careers','hr') from person-guessing.

recommendations: Build a domain->pattern KB service seeded from domain_kb.json (219 domains already characterized) and continuously updated with every address you confirm. On lookup: if dominant_shape share >=60%, generate only that exact template (store the literal template string, not just the shape family, because single_token can mean flast OR first OR last) and assign ~0.9 prior; else fall back to the generic ordered list.

Ship the generic ordered candidate list as the default: first.last, flast, first, firstlast, first.l, firstl, f.last, last.first, last, lastf, first_last, first-last, lastfirst, last.f — with the prior weights given above. Force dot as the primary separator (audit: 2535 dot vs 27 underscore vs 2 hyphen) and demote underscore/hyphen to near-zero unless the domain KB says otherwise.

Implement the token pipeline in strict order before permutation: NFKD + unidecode transliteration -> lowercase/strip punctuation & titles/suffixes -> nickname/diminutive bidirectional expansion -> multi-word/hyphenated surname expansion -> middle-name variants -> dedupe. Ship a bundled nickname table and a diacritics map; for non-Latin scripts keep top-2 romanizations.

Add India-specific rules as a first-class branch (audit is India-heavy): handle FirstName+Initial surnames (generate first.<init>, first<init>, first-only), single-name mononyms (first-only, don't fabricate a surname), and first+collapsed-initials patterns (e.g. saravanan.gm, apeksha.ss). Bump first.l up the ranking for .in / Indian-company domains (purplle.com evidence).

Compute a final 0-100 confidence = weighted blend of pattern_prior + domain_pattern_match + public_source_evidence + smtp_signal, where source evidence dominates and SMTP is gated by provider: decisive on Google Workspace/Proofpoint/Mimecast/Cisco IronPort, ignored-as-negative on Microsoft 365 (return top-prior candidate flagged 'unverifiable-M365' instead of downranking).

Generate the full set but verify in ranked order with short-circuit-on-first-accept; throttle RCPT per MX, reuse SMTP sessions, filter role accounts (careers/hr/info/engineering), and never bulk-send to all permutations.
caveats: The 76% first.last / dot-dominant distribution comes from THIS specific 3,006-address audit (large-enterprise + India-heavy cold-outreach list), so its priors are more first.last-skewed than the broader market; the generic Interseller 5M-company numbers (and heavy dependence on company size) should be blended in for domains outside the audit's profile, especially SMBs/startups where bare-first dominates.

The audit's shape distribution is measured over addresses that were TARGETED (already-guessed or sourced), not a random sample of true addresses, so it partly reflects the guessing tool that produced the list — there is survivorship/selection bias in the raw shape percentages.

Nickname and transliteration tables are inherently incomplete: unidecode is poor for Arabic/Chinese/Cyrillic romanization ambiguity, and diminutive mappings (Bob->Robert) are Anglocentric and add little for Indian names — budget for false candidates there.

Hunter/MailMeteor/Clearbit do not publish their exact ranking algorithms or full permutation lists (MailMeteor's '34 permutations' and Hunter's score formula are described only qualitatively), so the tool-behavior claims are from vendor docs/reviews, not source code.

Provider-gated SMTP reliability rests on the audit's own bounce evidence (118 M365 policy rejects vs 3 true not-founds); if a domain migrates providers or its MX changes, re-detect the provider from live MX before trusting or discarding SMTP results.

verification.dimension: Email pattern generation & ranking (candidate permutation + prior-probability ordering from name + domain)

verified_findings.claim: Two-tier ranking: a domain-learned house pattern outranks generic priors; fall back to global priors only when the domain is unknown.
verified_findings.evidence: KB confirms strong per-domain internal consistency: amadeus.com 203/209 first.last, purplle.com 34/34 first.l, opengov.com 60/60 single_token, trimble.com 21/25 first_last, veeam.com 13/18 f.last. Commercial tools (Hunter, MailMeteor) do learn org pattern from confirmed addresses and apply to new names — confirmed via vendor docs. This is the single biggest accuracy lever and the claim is sound.
verified_findings.correction: None. Only refinement: store the literal template AND the separator, not just the shape family — trimble.com single-handedly breaks the 'dot is universal' rule (it uses underscore: ajith_c, bharat_dwarkani), which is exactly why domain-KB override must carry the separator too.

verified_findings.claim: Store the exact template string for single_token domains because construction differs (flast vs first vs last).
verified_findings.evidence: opengov.com single_token = flast (abarbaria, achauhan, adesai = first-initial+last); visa.com single_token = flast (agautam, akhandelwal, asingh). Both are flast-form single tokens, not bare-first. Confirms the shape family 'single_token' is genuinely ambiguous and the literal template must be stored.

verified_findings.claim: Generic default order tuned to this audit: first.last dominant, dot near-universal separator; more first.last-skewed than the generic Interseller study.
verified_findings.evidence: summary.md: first.last 2313/3006=77%, single_token 421=14%, first.l 200=6.6%, first_last 22=0.7%, f.last 19=0.6%, first-last 2=0.07%. Separators: dot 2535, none 442, underscore 27, hyphen 2. Interseller 5M-company study verified live: 10,001+ employees = first.last 56.31%, flast 21.75% (finding's 56%/22% correct). Audit is indeed more first.last-skewed than Interseller, consistent with large-enterprise + India-heavy profile.
verified_findings.correction: Interseller sub-figures 'first 6.6% / first_last 3.6%' for 10001+ could not be independently confirmed (search returned only the top-two patterns) — minor and not load-bearing. The hand-assigned prior weights (0.60/0.12/...) are reasonable starting priors summing to ~1.0, but they are asserted defaults to be learned, not derived from the audit; treat as seed values.

verified_findings.claim: Token pipeline (NFKD/transliterate -> lowercase/strip titles/suffixes -> nickname expansion -> surname/middle expansion -> dedupe) must run BEFORE permutation.
verified_findings.evidence: Standard, correct pipeline; unidecode-style transliteration and bidirectional diminutive tables are the industry approach (MailMeteor permutator, unidecode). Ordering (as-given first, then formal, then diminutives) is sensible. No technical error.

verified_findings.claim: Middle/hyphenated/multi-word surnames need explicit expansion; Indian first+collapsed-initials (saravanan.gm, apeksha.ss) are a common pattern.
verified_findings.evidence: KB confirms the exact cited locals: harman.com 'saravanan.gm', amadeus.com 'apeksha.ss','bhargav.k','abhishek.kd'. These are genuinely first + collapsed remaining-name initials — a real and well-observed Indian pattern. Expansion rules are correct.

verified_findings.claim: India-specific rules are first-class: initial-based surnames, mononyms, first+initial; bump first.l up the ranking for .in / Indian-company domains.
verified_findings.evidence: The FirstName+Initial, mononym, and don't-fabricate-a-surname rules are well-grounded and correct (harman/amadeus KB evidence). BUT 'bump first.l for .in/Indian domains generally' rests on purplle.com alone. The audit's other Indian-founded companies are overwhelmingly first.last: chargebee.com (35 first.last vs 20 first.l), wingify.com (50/1), navi.com (48/0), tessell.com (41/0), easebuzz.in (37/3).
verified_findings.correction: Do NOT broadly bump first.l for all Indian/.in domains — that generalizes from a single outlier (purplle) and is contradicted by the majority of Indian companies in the audit, which are first.last dominant. Keep first.last as the Indian default; rely on the per-domain KB for first.l companies, and hold first.l only as a fallback (#2-3) rather than promoting it domain-class-wide.

verified_findings.claim: Scoring: source-evidence + domain-pattern-match dominate; SMTP RCPT is decisive on Google Workspace/Proofpoint/Mimecast/Cisco IronPort (honest 550 5.1.1) and must be ignored-as-negative on M365 (550 5.4.1 policy).
verified_findings.evidence: 550 5.1.1 = 'bad destination mailbox' = true negative (RFC 3463, confirmed). 550 5.4.1: RFC 3463 defines it as network/routing, but M365 repurposes it as 'Recipient address rejected: Access denied' (Microsoft support confirmed). Audit provider table confirms the asymmetry: M365 = 3 true not-founds vs 118 recipient_rejected; google_workspace = 175 honest not-founds; proofpoint 77; cisco 52; mimecast 15. Hunter score semantics confirmed live: ~95 = found in public source, ~60 = pattern-guessed. MailMeteor confirmed at 34 permutations with Valid/Risky labels. The conclusion (mark M365 unverifiable rather than downrank) is the safe, correct call.
verified_findings.correction: Refine the mechanism: 550 5.4.1 on M365 is AMBIGUOUS, not simply a 'policy block on valid mailboxes.' Microsoft's Directory-Based Edge Blocking emits 550 5.4.1 precisely when the recipient IS invalid, but the same code also fires for sender-side reputation/connection-filter blocks and blocked source domains — so you cannot tell invalid-mailbox from sender-blocked. That ambiguity (not 'it always fires on valid mailboxes') is why RCPT is untrustworthy on M365. 'RCPT probing is USELESS on M365' is slightly overstated — it is tenant-config-dependent (DBEB on/off), but treating M365 RCPT as unreliable is the correct default. Also note M365/Google catch-all/accept-all tenants return accepts for any local part, so a positive RCPT is not proof either; gate on catch-all detection first.

verified_findings.claim: Never spray full permutation set via SMTP; generate all, probe in ranked order, short-circuit on first accept, throttle per MX, reuse session, filter role accounts.
verified_findings.evidence: Standard deliverability best practice (MailMeteor and every reputable permutator warn against bulk-sending). KB confirms role locals to filter: hackerearth.com 'careers','hr'; akamai.com 'hiring'. Correct and implementation-ready. Add: 15-30% deliverability hit from spraying is corroborated by current vendor sources.

must_fix_errors: Recommendation 'bump first.l up the ranking for .in / Indian-company domains' overgeneralizes from purplle.com (the sole first.l Indian company). The audit's other Indian-founded companies are overwhelmingly first.last (chargebee 35/20, wingify 50/1, navi 48/0, tessell 41/0, easebuzz.in 37/3). Fix: keep first.last as the Indian default, rely on the per-domain KB for known first.l orgs, and hold first.l only as a #2-3 fallback — do not promote it domain-class-wide.

Reword the M365 SMTP claim: 550 5.4.1 is AMBIGUOUS, not 'a policy block that fires even for valid mailboxes.' Microsoft's Directory-Based Edge Blocking emits 550 5.4.1 for genuinely invalid recipients AND the same code fires for sender-reputation/connection-filter/blocked-domain reasons, so it cannot be trusted as a mailbox-existence signal in either direction. The conclusion (mark M365 unverifiable, don't downrank) stays; only the mechanism description needs correcting. Also add catch-all/accept-all detection before trusting any positive RCPT on Google/M365 tenants.

research.dimension: SMTP/MX verification mechanics (mailbox existence probing without sending mail)

key_findings.claim: The full probe sequence is: MX lookup -> A-record fallback -> TCP:25 connect -> read banner -> EHLO -> MAIL FROM:<probe> -> RCPT TO:<candidate> -> read code -> QUIT. DATA is never sent.
key_findings.detail: Step by step. (1) Resolve MX for the domain (DNS type MX), sort by ascending preference, and probe the LOWEST-preference (highest-priority) host first; keep the rest as fallbacks. (2) If NO MX records exist, RFC 5321 sec 5.1 'implicit MX' rule says fall back to the domain's A/AAAA record and treat that host as an MX of preference 0. If neither MX nor A resolves -> classify as dns_failure (this maps to the audit's 17 dns_failure bounces) and do not probe. (3) Open TCP to port 25 on the chosen host. (4) Read the multi-line 220 greeting banner before sending anything. (5) Send 'EHLO your.fqdn' (must be a real resolvable FQDN matching your PTR/rDNS, not 'localhost'); read the 250- capability list. Fall back to 'HELO your.fqdn' only if EHLO yields 5xx. (6) 'MAIL FROM:<probe@yourdomain.tld>' using a real, deliverable address on a domain you control with valid SPF/PTR - many servers reject empty <> or mismatched senders and some do sender-callback verification on YOUR probe address. (7) 'RCPT TO:<candidate@target.com>' - THIS reply is the verification signal. (8) Send 'QUIT' and close. Never send DATA. One TCP+EHLO+MAIL session can carry many sequential RCPT TO commands (connection reuse) to amortize handshake cost.

key_findings.claim: RCPT reply-code interpretation: 250/251 = accept; 550 5.1.1 = true not-found (hard negative); 550/553 5.4.1 & 5.7.x = policy block (INDETERMINATE, not a not-found); 450/451/421 = transient/greylist (retry).
key_findings.detail: Map the code + RFC 3463 enhanced status subcode, not just the 3-digit code. 250 = mailbox exists / will accept. 251 & 252 = will forward / cannot VRFY but will attempt (treat 252 as unknown). 550 5.1.1 'User unknown' / 'address_not_found' = mailbox does NOT exist -> hard fail (this is the audit's #1 bounce cause, 352 records, and IS trustworthy from Google Workspace, Proofpoint/pphosted, Mimecast, Cisco IronPort/iphmx). 550/553 5.4.1 'Access denied', and 5.7.1/5.7.x 'policy'/relaying-denied/blocked = the server refused for POLICY reasons and this fires even for VALID mailboxes -> classify INDETERMINATE, never as not-found. This is exactly the audit's Microsoft 365 (mx *.protection.outlook.com) pattern: 118 recipient_rejected 5.4.1 vs only 3 true 5.1.1, so RCPT probing cannot confirm/deny M365 mailboxes. 450 4.x.x / 451 4.7.1 = temporary/greylist deferral; 421 4.x.x = 'service not available, closing channel' (rate-limit / connection drop). All 4xx = retry later, do not conclude.

key_findings.claim: You must NOT send DATA - stopping at RCPT means no message is transmitted, avoiding spam/bounce liability; VRFY/EXPN are effectively useless because they are disabled almost everywhere.
key_findings.detail: Sending DATA (and BODY/end-of-data '.') actually DELIVERS the message. The whole point of verification is to learn existence WITHOUT delivering, so you QUIT after RCPT. Sending DATA would generate real mail, hurt sender reputation, and risk being flagged. VRFY <user> and EXPN <list> were the RFC-blessed existence commands but are disabled by default on modern servers (Exchange 2000+, hardened Postfix/Sendmail via 'disable_vrfy_command') precisely to stop address harvesting - so RCPT TO is the de-facto probe. Expect VRFY to return 252 'cannot verify' or 502 'command disabled'; do not rely on it.

key_findings.claim: CRITICAL DEPLOYMENT CONSTRAINT: outbound TCP port 25 is blocked by virtually all residential ISPs and by default on the major clouds (AWS EC2, Azure PAYG/free, GCP) - so RCPT verification is usually IMPOSSIBLE from a laptop or a default cloud VM.
key_findings.detail: Residential ISPs (Comcast/Xfinity, Verizon, AT&T, most others) block outbound :25 to curb spam. AWS EC2 blocks outbound :25 by default and has stopped granting unblock requests since 2020 (removal now routes through their SES/authorized-relay path). Azure blocks outbound :25 for Pay-As-You-Go, free-trial, and most subscription types and will not lift it except for some Enterprise Agreement/MCA-E subs. GCP blocks :25 on Compute Engine egress with no exceptions. Note port 587 (submission) and 465 are NOT substitutes for verification - they require AUTH and only let you send AS an authenticated user; they do not let you probe an arbitrary third-party MX. Verification genuinely needs raw outbound :25 to the target's MX. Symptom of a block: connect() hangs and times out (no RST), so treat connect-timeout as 'cannot verify from this host', not as 'mailbox invalid'.

key_findings.claim: Mitigations for the port-25 block: (a) run the prober on a VPS/provider that permits outbound :25, or (b) skip your own SMTP entirely and call a verification API (ZeroBounce/NeverBounce/Kickbox/Bouncer, ~$0.008/verify; MyEmailVerifier ~$0.0025).
key_findings.detail: Option A - self-host: providers that allow outbound :25 (often after a brief KYC/reputation check) include many bare-metal/VPS hosts such as Hetzner, OVH, Contabo, DigitalOcean (on request), Vultr (on request), and Scaleway; you still need clean IP reputation, valid PTR/rDNS matching your EHLO name, and SPF on the probe domain, or targets will 5xx/greylist you. Option B - outsource: verification APIs run the SMTP probing from their own warmed IP pools and return valid/invalid/catch-all/unknown JSON. 2026 list pricing is roughly $0.008/verification for ZeroBounce, NeverBounce, Kickbox, Bouncer; MyEmailVerifier ~$0.0025; ZeroBounce adds AI catch-all probability scoring. Option B is the pragmatic default because it also sidesteps IP-reputation, greylisting, and rate-limit management. A hybrid works well: use the deterministic domain_kb (patterns + provider) to shortlist candidates, then send only the ambiguous ones to the API.

key_findings.claim: Connection reuse, timeouts, retries, and rate-limiting are essential to avoid being throttled/blocked mid-run.
key_findings.detail: Reuse: after one EHLO+MAIL FROM you may issue multiple RCPT TO lines on the SAME connection (pipelining candidates for one domain), then QUIT - this is faster and lighter on the target, but many servers cap RCPTs-per-session and RCPTs-per-connection; on a 4xx 'too many recipients' or 421, back off and reconnect. Timeouts (RFC 5321 sec 4.5.3.2 minimums): connect ~ a few seconds up to ~30s; wait for the 220 banner up to ~5 min but practically 30s; per-command reply ~30-60s. A read timeout or connect timeout = 'unknown', never 'invalid'. Retries: on 421/450/451 (greylist) requeue and retry after 5-15 min (real MTAs retry over hours) - a valid mailbox behind greylisting returns 'unknown' on first contact. Rate limit yourself per destination IP/domain (small concurrency, spacing) or providers will tarpit or 421 you and can blocklist the probe IP.

key_findings.claim: Catch-all (accept-all) domains defeat RCPT probing entirely: they return 250 for every address, real or fake, so a 250 there proves nothing.
key_findings.detail: A catch-all/accept-all MX accepts RCPT TO for any local part at the domain. Detect it by probing a deliberately random, almost-certainly-nonexistent local part (e.g. 'zz-nonexistent-<rand>@domain'); if that returns 250, the domain is catch-all and you must downgrade every 250 for that domain to 'risky/unknown' rather than 'valid'. Separately, Google Workspace and Microsoft 365 frequently ACCEPT-then-bounce: they return 250 at RCPT regardless of mailbox existence and only generate the real 5.1.1/5.4.1 asynchronously as a bounce - which is why the audit could only classify M365/Google outcomes from actual bounce DSNs, not from a live RCPT 250. Practical resolution tiers: VALID (honest-provider 250 + not catch-all), INVALID (550 5.1.1), CATCH-ALL/RISKY (catch-all 250, or M365 250), UNKNOWN (4xx/timeout/policy 5.4.1). This tier system should feed directly into the finder's confidence score alongside the pattern-frequency evidence in domain_kb.json.

recommendations: Do not build RCPT probing as the primary verifier for Microsoft 365 domains (mx *.protection.outlook.com). The audit proves M365 returns 550 5.4.1 policy blocks for valid mailboxes and almost never honest 5.1.1 - route M365 domains to pattern-confidence + API/bounce-based confirmation instead. In domain_kb.json, 46 M365 domains / 810 addresses fall in this bucket.

Enable RCPT probing ONLY for provider classes the audit showed give honest 550 5.1.1: Google Workspace, Proofpoint (pphosted.com), Mimecast, Cisco IronPort (iphmx.com). Gate the prober on the domain's detected provider (already stored per-domain in domain_kb.json).

Assume you cannot probe from the app host. Architect verification as a separate worker on a VPS with outbound :25 allowed + clean PTR/rDNS/SPF, OR call a verification API (ZeroBounce/NeverBounce ~$0.008, MyEmailVerifier ~$0.0025). Never attempt raw :25 from a laptop or default AWS/Azure/GCP VM.

Always run a random-local-part catch-all probe per domain before trusting any 250; cache the catch-all verdict per domain (add a 'catch_all' boolean to domain_kb.json) so you probe it once, not per address.

Classify results into 4 tiers - VALID / INVALID (550 5.1.1) / RISKY (catch-all or M365 250) / UNKNOWN (4xx, timeout, 5.4.1/5.7.x policy) - and combine with the pattern-frequency prior from the audit rather than treating SMTP as binary truth.
Implement greylist handling: on 421/450/451, requeue and retry after 5-15 minutes (up to a few attempts over hours) before emitting UNKNOWN; a first-contact temp-reject is not a failure.

Use a real resolvable EHLO FQDN and a real deliverable MAIL FROM probe address on a domain you control (valid SPF + matching PTR), reuse one connection for multiple RCPTs per domain, and rate-limit per destination to avoid tarpitting/blocklisting.
caveats: SMTP RCPT verification is inherently probabilistic, not authoritative: honest providers can still greylist, rate-limit, or change policy, and accept-then-bounce providers (Google, M365) make a live 250 unreliable - the audit had to derive M365/Google truth from bounce DSNs, not live probes.

Pricing figures ($0.008 ZeroBounce/NeverBounce, $0.0025 MyEmailVerifier) are 2026 list prices from vendor/comparison blogs and change with volume tiers; confirm on the vendor's current pricing page before committing.

The exact set of VPS providers that permit outbound :25 changes and usually requires a reputation/KYC step; verify with the specific host before relying on it. AWS/Azure/GCP defaults (blocked) are stable and documented.

Aggressive probing risks getting your IP blocklisted and can itself look like a directory-harvest attack (DHA); some providers deliberately return misleading 250s or tarpit probers, so treating probing as low-volume and provider-gated is a correctness as well as an etiquette requirement.
I did not read all 7,681 lines of domain_kb.json (paged view); provider/MX attributions cited come from summary.md and the first ~50 domains, which are consistent with the stated ground-truth facts.

verification.dimension: SMTP/MX verification mechanics (mailbox existence probing without sending mail)

verified_findings.claim: Full probe sequence: MX lookup -> A-record implicit-MX fallback -> TCP:25 -> banner -> EHLO -> MAIL FROM -> RCPT TO -> read code -> QUIT; DATA never sent; probe lowest-preference MX first.
verified_findings.evidence: Matches RFC 5321: sec 5.1 defines implicit MX (fall back to A/AAAA as preference-0 host when no MX), sec 5.1 mandates sorting MX by ascending preference and trying lowest-numbered (highest-priority) first, and the RCPT-then-QUIT (no DATA) pattern is the standard existence-probe technique. Sequential RCPT reuse per connection is valid (RFC 5321 allows multiple RCPT per transaction).
verified_findings.correction: Minor: mapping A-record-fallback failures specifically to 'the audit's 17 dns_failure bounces' is an illustrative over-attribution — dns_failure (17 in summary.md) can also cover NXDOMAIN, SERVFAIL, or resolution timeouts, not only missing-MX-and-A. Treat the mapping as approximate, not one-to-one.

verified_findings.claim: RCPT reply-code interpretation: 250/251=accept; 550 5.1.1=true not-found; 550/553 5.4.1 & 5.7.x=policy (indeterminate); 4xx (421/450/451)=transient/greylist (retry).
verified_findings.evidence: Consistent with RFC 5321 sec 4.2 (2xx success, 4xx transient, 5xx permanent) and RFC 3463 enhanced subcodes. Reconciles exactly with audit summary.md: Google Workspace/Proofpoint/Mimecast/Cisco IronPort produced honest 550 5.1.1 address_not_found (175/77/15/52 respectively), while Microsoft 365 produced 118 recip_rejected (5.4.1) vs only 3 true 5.1.1 — confirming 5.4.1 is a policy block, not a not-found.
verified_findings.correction: Two RFC-purity nits (do not change the operational conclusion): (a) 5.4.1 per RFC 3463 literally means 'No answer from host' (5.4.x = network/routing); Microsoft repurposes '550 5.4.1 Access denied' for recipient policy rejection — the finding describes observed M365 behavior correctly but it is a vendor reinterpretation, not the RFC meaning. (b) 553 per RFC is 'mailbox name not allowed' (syntax/naming), slightly distinct from generic policy blocking. 252 correctly treated as unknown.

verified_findings.claim: Do NOT send DATA (stopping at RCPT transmits no message); VRFY/EXPN are useless because disabled almost everywhere.
verified_findings.evidence: RFC 5321 sec 3.3: message is only delivered after DATA + end-of-data '.'; QUIT-after-RCPT delivers nothing. VRFY/EXPN (RFC 5321 sec 3.5) are widely disabled on modern MTAs (Exchange, hardened Postfix via disable_vrfy_command) to prevent address harvesting, typically returning 252 or 502 — so RCPT TO is the de-facto probe. Standard, accurate.

verified_findings.claim: Outbound TCP:25 is blocked by virtually all residential ISPs and by default on AWS EC2, Azure PAYG/free, and GCP — RCPT verification usually impossible from laptop or default cloud VM. Port 587/465 are not substitutes (require AUTH). Connect-timeout = 'cannot verify', not 'invalid'.
verified_findings.evidence: AWS re:Post confirms EC2 throttles/blocks outbound :25 by default requiring a removal request (repost.aws/knowledge-center/ec2-port-25-throttle, current as of 2026 search). GCP never permits :25 egress; Azure blocks :25 for PAYG/free/most subscription types (Microsoft Learn). 587/465 are submission ports requiring AUTH — they only let you send AS an authenticated user, not probe a third-party MX. Connect-timeout-as-unknown is correct handling.
verified_findings.correction: Small wording caveat: AWS's own docs frame the removal path as 'request removal of email-sending limitations,' and successful unblocks still happen (they are just gated by review), so 'has stopped granting unblock requests since 2020' is slightly overstated — the practical guidance (assume blocked; do not rely on it) is sound.

verified_findings.claim: Mitigations: (a) VPS/provider permitting outbound :25 (Hetzner/OVH/Contabo/DO/Vultr/Scaleway) with clean PTR/rDNS/SPF, or (b) verification API — ZeroBounce/NeverBounce/Kickbox/Bouncer ~$0.008/verify, MyEmailVerifier ~$0.0025.
verified_findings.evidence: 2026 pricing confirmed: ZeroBounce $0.008/request, NeverBounce $0.008, Kickbox flat $0.008 (up to $0.01 by volume tier), Bouncer in same band; MyEmailVerifier ~$0.0025 (myemailverifier.com/blog/email-verification-api-pricing). List prices vary with volume tiers — confirm on vendor page before committing, as the finding's own caveat notes.
verified_findings.correction: The VPS-allows-:25 list is directionally right but volatile and KYC/reputation-gated per host (DO/Vultr explicitly 'on request'); verify with the specific provider rather than treating the list as guaranteed.

verified_findings.claim: Connection reuse, timeouts (RFC 5321 minimums), retries on 421/450/451, and per-destination rate-limiting are essential; read/connect timeout = unknown, never invalid.
verified_findings.evidence: RFC 5321 sec 4.5.3.2 specifies timeout minimums (initial 220 banner 5 min, MAIL/RCPT 5 min, per-command generally ~5 min minimums — the finding's 30-60s practical values are tighter but operationally reasonable). Greylisting genuinely returns 4xx on first contact then accepts on retry, so requeue-and-retry is correct. Multiple RCPT per session and per-domain rate-limiting to avoid tarpit/DHA-flagging are standard best practice.

verified_findings.claim: Catch-all domains defeat RCPT probing (250 for everything); detect via random-local-part probe. Additionally, 'Google Workspace and Microsoft 365 frequently ACCEPT-then-bounce: return 250 at RCPT regardless of mailbox existence.' Tier system: VALID / INVALID(550 5.1.1) / RISKY(catch-all or M365 250) / UNKNOWN(4xx/timeout/5.4.1).
verified_findings.evidence: The catch-all mechanic and random-local-part detection are correct and standard. But the accept-then-bounce sub-claim is inaccurate and internally contradictory. (1) It contradicts this same report's earlier claim (and the audit) that Google Workspace gives honest 550 5.1.1 at RCPT — Google produced 175 honest address_not_found in the audit and is listed as a trustworthy RCPT provider. Google Workspace normally rejects unknown recipients at RCPT time with 550 5.1.1 UNLESS a catch-all is configured; it is NOT a blanket accept-then-bounce provider. (2) For Microsoft 365 the audit shows the dominant live-RCPT behavior is rejection with 550 5.4.1 (118 recip_rejected) — i.e. a synchronous policy REJECT, not a 250-accept-then-async-bounce. So characterizing M365 primarily as '250 accept-then-bounce' mislabels the audited signal.
verified_findings.correction: Rephrase: Microsoft 365/Exchange Online is the accept-then-bounce / tarpit-with-5.4.1 provider (behavior varies by tenant config: some tarpit-reject at RCPT with 5.4.1, some accept 250 then async-bounce) — either way its live 250 is unreliable and its 5.4.1 is not a not-found. Google Workspace generally gives an honest synchronous RCPT verdict (550 5.1.1 for not-found, 250 for real) EXCEPT on catch-all domains; do not lump Google into 'accept-then-bounce.' Keep the RISKY tier for catch-all-250 and for M365 250s, but drop the assertion that Google 250s are inherently unreliable — downgrade a Google 250 only when the random-local-part probe proves the domain is catch-all.

verified_findings.claim: MAIL FROM must use a real deliverable address on a domain you control (valid SPF/PTR); 'many servers reject empty <> or mismatched senders.'
verified_findings.evidence: The operational advice (use a real, resolvable EHLO FQDN and a real deliverable MAIL FROM address on an SPF/PTR-clean domain) is correct and best practice, because some MTAs perform sender-address-verification callbacks. However the justification 'many servers reject empty <>' is overstated: the null sender MAIL FROM:<> is RFC 5321-mandated to be accepted (it is the required envelope for DSNs/bounces), so a blanket claim that many servers reject <> is not accurate.
verified_findings.correction: Keep the recommendation to use a real deliverable probe address — but justify it by sender-callback-verification (SAV) and reputation, not by 'servers reject <>.' The null sender is standards-legitimate; the real reason to avoid it is that some anti-spam systems treat callback/bounce-shaped probes suspiciously and SAV needs a real address to call back to.

must_fix_errors: Catch-all section: the claim that Google Workspace 'frequently ACCEPT-then-bounce... return 250 at RCPT regardless of mailbox existence' is wrong and contradicts the report's own (audit-backed) finding that Google gives honest 550 5.1.1. Google normally rejects unknown recipients synchronously at RCPT; it is only unreliable on catch-all domains. Remove Google from the accept-then-bounce category.

Microsoft 365 is mischaracterized as '250 accept-then-bounce'; the audit's dominant M365 live-RCPT signal is a synchronous 550 5.4.1 policy reject (118 records) — reframe M365 as tarpit/policy-reject-or-accept-then-bounce (config-dependent) so the tier logic keys off provider correctly.

research.dimension: Catch-all (accept-all) domain detection & greylisting for SMTP-based email verification

key_findings.claim: Catch-all detection = RCPT a guaranteed-nonexistent random local part; a 250 means per-address RCPT verification is meaningless for that domain.
key_findings.detail: Open a normal SMTP session (HELO/EHLO with a real FQDN, MAIL FROM a real domain you control, MAIL FROM <> null sender is riskier because some servers reject it), then RCPT TO a local part that cannot exist. Generate it to be collision-proof AND obviously fake: e.g. a 20-char random base32 string plus a timestamp, like 'zzq7x8k3n2p9-noexist-1721300000@domain'. If the server returns 2xx (250/251) for that, the domain accepts all recipients -> it is catch-all / accept-all. If it returns 550 5.1.1 (or 5.1.10) 'address not found', the server does honest per-recipient validation and you CAN trust an RCPT probe of the real address. Use a single probe to classify, but confirm with 2-3 more distinct random probes before finalizing 'catch-all' to defend against transient 250s and per-connection quirks. If probes disagree (some 250, some 550), treat as 'unknown/inconsistent', not catch-all.

key_findings.claim: On Microsoft 365 (mx *.protection.outlook.com), BOTH RCPT verification AND catch-all detection are unreliable — do not RCPT-probe M365 at all.
key_findings.detail: Your own audit proves it: M365 returned 550 5.4.1 'Access denied' (recipient_rejected) for 118 addresses regardless of mailbox existence and only 3 honest 550 5.1.1 not-founds. Symmetrically, M365 EOP frequently accepts every RCPT at the edge (250) and defers real validation to after-DATA or internal routing ('deferred rejection'), so a 250 on M365 does NOT prove catch-all and a 550 5.4.1 does NOT prove not-found. Net rule: if MX matches *.protection.outlook.com (or *.mail.protection.outlook.com), SKIP SMTP probing entirely and label the address 'pattern-based only' (aka 'unknown' from SMTP's perspective), scoring purely from your domain_kb pattern evidence. 46 M365 domains / 810 addresses in your audit fall in this bucket.

key_findings.claim: Catch-all/RCPT probing is trustworthy on the providers that returned honest 550 5.1.1 in the audit: Google Workspace, Proofpoint, Mimecast, Cisco IronPort.
key_findings.detail: These providers do real per-recipient validation at RCPT time (audit: google 175 addr_not_found, proofpoint 77, ironport 52, mimecast 15 — all honest 5.1.1). For these, run the catch-all probe; if honest-reject, a subsequent RCPT of the real address gives a trustworthy valid/invalid. Note providers still tarpit and rate-limit (see below), and some Google Workspace tenants ARE configured catch-all — so detection is per-domain, never per-provider. IronPort/Proofpoint/Mimecast are gateways that sit in front of the real mail store; they may validate against a synced recipient list (honest) OR be configured to accept-all and let the backend bounce — the random-probe test still correctly distinguishes the two configs.

key_findings.claim: Greylisting shows as a transient 4xx (421 or 451, usually 451 4.7.1) on the FIRST contact from an unknown IP+MAIL FROM+RCPT triplet; it is NOT invalid — retry after a delay.
key_findings.detail: RFC 5321 4.2.1: 4yz = transient, same command may succeed on retry. Greylisters key on the triplet (sender IP, envelope sender, recipient) and defer unknown triplets. 451 4.7.1 is the classic greylist signal; 421 'service not available, closing channel' also appears (some servers, e.g. SmarterMail, greylist with 421). Correct handling: on any 4xx during MAIL/RCPT, classify 'greylisted/deferred', do NOT mark invalid, and queue a retry. Greylist windows are typically 1-15 min minimum with the deferral expiring after hours; practical retry schedule: wait ~60-90s then retry, then exponential backoff (e.g. 1min, 5min, 15min, 60min) up to a cap (e.g. 4-6 attempts over a few hours). Reuse the SAME source IP and SAME MAIL FROM across retries so the triplet matches and the greylist clears. If still 4xx after the cap, output 'unknown' (not invalid).

key_findings.claim: Distinguish greylisting from tarpitting from rate-limit/blocklisting by timing and code, because each demands different verifier behavior.
key_findings.detail: Greylisting = clean 4xx returned reasonably fast, clears on retry with same triplet. Tarpitting = the server deliberately DELAYS its response (multi-second pause after RCPT/DATA) rather than rejecting — you get a slow 250/450; handle with generous socket timeouts (e.g. 30-60s) and low concurrency, do not hammer. Rate-limiting = 421/451 4.7.x with reputation wording (e.g. Microsoft '451 4.7.651 temporarily rate limited due to IP reputation', or '4.7.28'); back off hard, lower connection concurrency (Microsoft says <500 simultaneous conns), and pace probes per destination. Blocklisting = immediate rejection/drop right after connect or on HELO (often 5xx 'blocked'/'access denied' or connection reset) BEFORE you send RCPT — this is about YOUR prober IP, not the address; results are worthless from that IP, rotate IP and mark address 'unknown'. Spamhaus PBL specifically lists dynamic/end-user IP ranges that must not speak SMTP directly, so probe only from static IPs with matching PTR/rDNS and correct HELO.

key_findings.claim: Commercial verifiers converge on 5 result labels — map your output to them: valid/deliverable, invalid, catch-all/accept-all, unknown, risky (do-not-mail).
key_findings.detail: ZeroBounce: valid, invalid, catch-all, unknown, spamtrap, abuse, do_not_mail (+ sub-reasons). NeverBounce: valid, invalid, disposable, catch-all (accept-all), unknown. Hunter: deliverable/undeliverable/risky with accept_all + score. Definitions: VALID = honest 250 on the real address at an honest-validating server. INVALID = honest 550 5.1.1/5.1.10 on the real address at an honest server. CATCH-ALL/ACCEPT-ALL = random probe got 250, so the specific address cannot be confirmed by SMTP (deliverability real but unproven). UNKNOWN = timeout/greylist-not-cleared/tarpit/rate-limited/blocklisted/M365 policy — verification could not run. RISKY/DO-NOT-MAIL = role-based (info@, sales@), disposable domain, spamtrap, known-complainer, or accept-all — a policy overlay on top of the raw SMTP result. Keep the raw SMTP outcome and the risk overlay as separate fields so you can re-derive labels.

key_findings.claim: Catch-all must downgrade the confidence score to 'pattern-based only' — the SMTP layer contributes nothing, so the score reduces to your audit's pattern priors.
key_findings.detail: Concrete scoring model (0-100). Base pattern score from domain_kb: if the guessed local part matches the domain's dominant shape (e.g. first.last where domain is 76%-dominant) start high (~75-85), lower for rarer shapes; hard-penalize if local part is in known_bad_locals (->invalid). SMTP modifier: (a) honest server + 250 on real address -> valid, cap 95-98; (b) honest server + 550 5.1.1 -> invalid, score ~2; (c) CATCH-ALL detected -> SMTP adds/subtracts nothing: final = pattern score only, and hard-cap it at ~55-60 and label 'catch-all / pattern-based only' (never 'valid'); (d) unknown/greylist-uncleared/M365/rate-limited/blocklisted -> also pattern-only, cap ~50, label 'unknown'. Because your audit's #1 bounce cause is address_not_found (wrong guess) and catch-all removes the only signal that catches wrong guesses, catch-all domains carry real bounce risk even at a good pattern match — reflect that with the cap. Optionally raise a catch-all address a few points if MULTIPLE independent sources corroborate the exact local part (e.g. found on LinkedIn + a second source), since pattern-only is your sole evidence there.

key_findings.claim: Cache the domain-level catch-all/provider verdict; it changes rarely and probing repeatedly wastes reputation and invites blocklisting.
key_findings.detail: Persist per domain: provider (from MX), is_catch_all (tri-state: yes/no/unknown), last_probed_at, honest_reject_confirmed (bool), tarpit/rate-limit flags. TTL ~7-30 days for catch-all status, refresh lazily. This means for a batch you probe each DOMAIN's catch-all status ~once, then verify individual addresses only on honest, non-catch-all, non-M365 domains. It also lets you pre-classify the whole batch cheaply and only spend SMTP budget where it produces signal (per audit: Google/Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort domains that are not catch-all).

recommendations: Full per-address pipeline: (1) MX lookup -> derive provider. (2) If MX = *.protection.outlook.com: SKIP SMTP, output 'pattern-based only'/unknown, score = pattern prior capped ~50-55. (3) Else look up cached domain catch-all verdict; if stale/missing, run catch-all probe: 1 random local part, and if 250 confirm with 2 more distinct randoms. (4) If catch-all -> label catch-all, score = pattern prior hard-capped ~55-60. (5) If honest-reject -> RCPT the real address: 250=valid(cap95), 550 5.1.x=invalid. (6) Any 4xx at any step -> greylist/deferred, retry with backoff (60s,5m,15m,60m) reusing same IP+MAIL FROM; if still 4xx after cap -> unknown. (7) Overlay risk flags (role-based, disposable, known_bad_locals) to produce final label.

Prober hygiene to avoid tarpit/blocklist: use dedicated static IPs with correct PTR/rDNS and matching HELO/EHLO FQDN, valid SPF; keep per-destination concurrency low (Microsoft: <500 simultaneous, but for probing go far lower — a few conns/domain); pace RCPTs; reuse one connection for HELO+catch-all-probe+real-address RCPT (fewer connects = less suspicion); rotate a small IP pool and check IPs against Spamhaus PBL/DBL; set 30-60s socket timeouts to survive tarpitting.

Treat the catch-all probe and the real-address RCPT in the SAME SMTP session where possible: HELO once, MAIL FROM once, then RCPT random (catch-all test) then RCPT real. This halves connections and makes the comparison apples-to-apples (same triplet, same server mood), improving reliability of the 250-vs-550 distinction.

Store SMTP result and risk overlay as separate fields, plus keep the raw response code+enhanced status (e.g. '550 5.1.1') and the exact random local part probed, so verdicts are auditable and you can reclassify later without re-probing (mirrors how you built domain_kb from real bounce codes).

Never emit 'valid' for a catch-all or M365 address — the strongest honest label is 'accept-all / pattern-based, unverified'. Reserve 'valid' for honest-server 250s. This directly addresses your audit's #1 bounce cause (wrong-guess address_not_found), which catch-all domains hide entirely.
caveats: Catch-all detection has false positives from 'deferred rejection' / after-DATA validation servers (notably M365 and some gateways) that 250 every RCPT but bounce later — the multi-random-probe test reduces but cannot eliminate this; the only certain catch-all test is sending a real message and watching for a bounce, which you are trying to avoid.

Provider-specific claims (M365 useless, Google/Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort honest) are grounded in YOUR 3,006-address audit and general provider behavior; individual tenants can be configured against type (a Google Workspace domain can be catch-all; an IronPort gateway can accept-all), so always verify per-domain, never assume from provider alone.

Greylisting retry timing varies widely (minimum defer 1-15 min; deferral record lifetime hours to days). A verifier that must return synchronously in seconds cannot fully clear greylisting — such addresses will land in 'unknown' unless you support async/deferred re-checking. Budget for a retry queue if you want to resolve them.

MAIL FROM choice matters: a null sender <> is more standards-correct for probing but is rejected by some servers and can trigger stricter greylisting; a real, warmed, SPF-aligned sender domain is generally more reliable but ties results to that domain's reputation. Test both against your target provider mix.

SMTP RCPT probing sits in a legal/ToS grey area (some providers' ToS prohibit verification probing; volume probing can look like a directory-harvest/DoS). Rate-limit conservatively and be prepared to fall back to pattern-only scoring for hostile providers rather than escalate probing.

Enhanced status codes are not perfectly standardized across servers: some return generic 550 without 5.1.1, some misuse 4xx/5xx. Parse defensively — treat any 5.1.x/5.5.0-with-'unknown user' text as not-found, any 4xx as transient, and unrecognized responses as 'unknown' rather than guessing.

verification.dimension: Catch-all (accept-all) domain detection & greylisting for SMTP-based email verification

verified_findings.claim: Catch-all detection = RCPT a guaranteed-nonexistent random local part; a 250 means per-address RCPT verification is meaningless for that domain. Confirm with 2-3 distinct random probes; inconsistent results = unknown, not catch-all.
verified_findings.evidence: This is the standard industry technique (BillionVerify, Scravio, Enrichley, ZeroBounce, NeverBounce all describe the random-RCPT probe). RFC 5321 §3.5.3 explicitly warns that RCPT/VRFY validation is unreliable precisely because many servers accept-all, which is the premise of the test. Multi-probe confirmation to defend against transient 250s is sound.
verified_findings.correction: One nuance the detail understates: a random-probe 250 does NOT distinguish a true catch-all from a server that 250s at the edge and rejects after DATA (see the M365 caveat). The multi-probe test reduces per-connection flukes but cannot detect deferred/after-DATA rejection. The finding's own caveats acknowledge this, so it is not a defect, but the 'guaranteed' framing in the claim title is slightly strong.

verified_findings.claim: On Microsoft 365 (*.protection.outlook.com) both RCPT verification AND catch-all detection are unreliable; skip SMTP probing entirely and score pattern-only. 46 M365 domains / 810 addresses in the audit.
verified_findings.evidence: Reconciles exactly with the audit: summary.md shows microsoft365 = 46 domains / 810 addrs, 118 recipient_rejected (550 5.4.1 Access denied) vs only 3 honest address_not_found. All 46 domain_kb entries checked show mx = *.mail.protection.outlook.com. The 5.4.1 'Access denied' on valid mailboxes is EOP's anti-directory-harvest / tarpit behavior, which is exactly why probing is useless. General provider behavior corroborates.
verified_findings.correction: Two overstatements to soften, not refute: (1) M365 is NOT monolithic — tenants with Directory Based Edge Blocking (DBEB) enabled DO honestly reject unknown recipients at RCPT, typically with 550 5.1.10 (RESOLVER.ADR.RecipientNotFound) or 5.1.1; the audit's own 3 honest not-founds are these. So a 5.1.1/5.1.10 from M365 IS trustworthy as invalid. (2) The claim lists only 5.1.1 as the honest not-found code and omits 5.1.10, which is the more common M365 not-found enhanced code. Net: 'skip probing, treat as unknown' is the correct CONSERVATIVE default given the 118:3 policy-block ratio, but the rule should still honor a clean 5.1.1/5.1.10 as invalid rather than discarding it.

verified_findings.claim: RCPT/catch-all probing is trustworthy on Google Workspace, Proofpoint, Mimecast, Cisco IronPort (audit honest 550 5.1.1); detection is per-domain not per-provider (some Google tenants are catch-all; gateways may accept-all).
verified_findings.evidence: Matches audit counts: google 175 addr_not_found, proofpoint 77, ironport 52, mimecast 15 — all honest 5.1.1. domain_kb shows real known_bad_locals populated for google (chargebee 21, tessell 9, navi 5, easebuzz 7, h2o 7), proofpoint (invesco 18, ge 10, akamai 6), ironport (harman 39), mimecast (ukg 9), i.e. these providers surfaced true per-recipient negatives. The per-domain (not per-provider) caveat is correct and important.
verified_findings.correction: Minor: these providers are 'predominantly honest,' not 100% honest. The same audit shows google_workspace also returned 10 recipient_rejected + 10 inactive_account, and cisco_ironport 3 recipient_rejected (harman) — so even on honest providers you will see non-5.1.1 responses that must be handled as policy/unknown, not invalid. The random-probe-per-domain approach the claim recommends handles this correctly.

verified_findings.claim: Greylisting shows as transient 4xx (usually 451 4.7.1, sometimes 421) on first contact from an unknown IP+MAIL FROM+RCPT triplet; not invalid — retry with same IP+MAIL FROM, backoff, and if still 4xx after cap output unknown.
verified_findings.evidence: RFC 5321 §4.2.1 defines 4yz as transient ('the command was not accepted, and the requested action did not occur... the sender-SMTP is encouraged to try again'). 451 4.7.1 is the canonical greylist signal (Axway, iRedMail, emailwarmup, warmy all confirm); 421 greylisting exists on some servers (SmarterMail). The triplet definition (sender IP + envelope sender + recipient) is correct, as is the requirement to reuse the same IP+MAIL FROM so the triplet matches on retry. Typical clear window 5-30 min confirmed.
verified_findings.correction: None material. The cited RFC section (§4.2.1, reply-code severities) is the correct citation for '4yz = transient.' The retry cadence (60s, 5m, 15m, 60m) is a reasonable engineering choice, not an RFC requirement.

verified_findings.claim: Distinguish greylisting vs tarpitting vs rate-limit/blocklisting by timing and code; Microsoft rate-limits with 451 4.7.x reputation wording, recommends <500 simultaneous connections; blocklisting (Spamhaus PBL) hits before RCPT and means rotate IP + mark unknown.
verified_findings.evidence: Microsoft's documented rate-limit codes verified via learn.microsoft.com Q&A: 451 4.7.500 'Server busy', 451 4.7.650 / 4.7.651 IP-reputation rate limiting, plus 4.7.28. Microsoft's published guidance is not to open >500 simultaneous connections to its inbound servers — confirmed. Spamhaus PBL is correctly described as listing dynamic/end-user ranges that must not speak SMTP directly; probing needs static IP + matching PTR/rDNS + correct HELO. Tarpit (deliberate slow response, needs generous timeouts + low concurrency) is accurately characterized.
verified_findings.correction: Small precision fix: the single most common Microsoft deferral is 451 4.7.500 'Server busy' — the detail leads with 4.7.651 and 4.7.28, which are real but less frequently seen than 4.7.500/4.7.650. Treat the whole 4.7.5xx–4.7.6xx family as rate-limit/reputation transient. Also note 451 4.7.500 itself is sometimes triggered by IP-reputation graylisting, so timing alone will not always cleanly separate greylist from rate-limit on M365 — code family is the more reliable discriminator.

verified_findings.claim: Commercial verifiers converge on ~5 labels — valid/deliverable, invalid, catch-all/accept-all, unknown, risky/do-not-mail — and you should keep raw SMTP outcome and risk overlay as separate fields.
verified_findings.evidence: ZeroBounce docs: valid, invalid, catch-all, unknown, spamtrap, abuse, do_not_mail (+ substatuses like mailbox_not_found, mail_server_did_not_respond, greylisted). NeverBounce: valid, invalid, disposable, catchall, unknown (verified on neverbounce.com/help/result-codes; unknown addresses retried up to 75x). Hunter: deliverable / undeliverable / risky with accept_all flag + confidence score (0-100), verified on help.hunter.io. The definitions map cleanly to honest-250=valid, honest-5.1.x=invalid, random-probe-250=catch-all, timeout/greylist/M365=unknown. Separating raw SMTP code from risk overlay is best practice and mirrors how domain_kb was built from raw bounce codes.
verified_findings.correction: None. Definitions are accurate. Worth noting NeverBounce uses exactly 5 core codes (no separate spamtrap/abuse) while ZeroBounce splits risk into more substatuses — so 'converge on 5' is a fair generalization, with ZeroBounce being the most granular.

verified_findings.claim: Catch-all must downgrade confidence to 'pattern-based only' (SMTP contributes nothing); score reduces to audit pattern priors, hard-capped ~55-60, never labeled 'valid'.
verified_findings.evidence: Logically sound and directly supported by the audit: the #1 bounce cause is address_not_found (352, wrong guess), and catch-all removes the exact SMTP signal that catches wrong guesses — so a good pattern match on a catch-all domain still carries real bounce risk. Hunter's own model corroborates: for accept_all it falls back to a confidence score rather than 'deliverable'. Capping and refusing 'valid' for catch-all/M365 is the correct conservative policy.
verified_findings.correction: The specific numbers (base 75-85 for dominant shape, cap 55-60 for catch-all, cap 50 for unknown, valid cap 95-98) are reasonable engineering defaults, not empirically derived from the audit — treat them as tunable, not established fact. The audit gives you priors to calibrate against (e.g. first.last = 76% globally), but the exact cap values should be validated against held-out bounce outcomes.

verified_findings.claim: Cache the domain-level catch-all/provider verdict (provider, is_catch_all tri-state, last_probed_at, honest_reject_confirmed, tarpit/rate-limit flags); TTL ~7-30 days, probe each domain's status ~once per batch.
verified_findings.evidence: Sound operational practice — repeated probing wastes IP reputation and invites blocklisting (consistent with Spamhaus PBL/rate-limit findings above). Provider/MX and catch-all config change rarely, so caching is appropriate. The tri-state (yes/no/unknown) correctly avoids collapsing 'not-yet-probed' into 'not-catch-all'.
verified_findings.correction: The 7-30 day TTL is a judgment call (the finding self-rates this 'medium' confidence, correctly). Catch-all status can change when a domain reconfigures its gateway (e.g. enabling/disabling DBEB on M365, or a Google tenant toggling a catch-all routing rule), so lazy refresh is right but the TTL should be shortened for domains where verification results start disagreeing with the cached verdict.

verified_findings.claim: MAIL FROM <> (null sender) is riskier than a real controlled domain because some servers reject it; prefer a real, warmed, SPF-aligned sender for probing.
verified_findings.evidence: Partially true but contestable. It is correct that some servers reject <> for non-DSN traffic and that greylisters can treat <> more harshly. However, a large share of production verifiers deliberately use MAIL FROM:<> precisely because it mimics a bounce/DSN probe and, critically, does NOT tie the probe's reputation to (or risk blocklisting) a real sending domain. RFC 5321 §4.5.5 requires servers to accept <> for DSNs.
verified_findings.correction: Reframe as a genuine tradeoff rather than a one-sided 'null sender is riskier': null sender = better reputation isolation but higher outright-rejection/greylist rate on some servers; real warmed sender = higher acceptance but couples results to (and can burn) that domain's reputation. The finding's own caveats already state this correctly — the headline detail overstates the case against <>. Recommend A/B testing both against the actual provider mix (M365/Google/Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort) as the caveat suggests.

must_fix_errors: M365 rule is slightly too absolute: add a carve-out that a clean 550 5.1.1 OR 550 5.1.10 (RESOLVER.ADR.RecipientNotFound, the common M365 not-found code the finding omits) from an M365 host IS a trustworthy 'invalid'. Tenants with Directory Based Edge Blocking (DBEB) reject unknown recipients honestly at RCPT — that is exactly the source of the audit's 3 honest M365 not-founds. Keep 'skip/treat-as-unknown' as the conservative default, but do not discard an honest not-found when M365 gives one.

Correct the Microsoft rate-limit code emphasis: the most common deferral is 451 4.7.500 'Server busy' (not the 4.7.651 / 4.7.28 the detail leads with). Handle the whole 4.7.5xx-4.7.6xx family as rate-limit/reputation transient; do not rely on timing alone to separate greylist from rate-limit on M365 since 4.7.500 can itself be reputation-driven graylisting.

research.dimension: Provider-specific behavior & MX classification for email verification

key_findings.claim: Classify the mail provider by matching MX hostname suffixes; a security-gateway MX (Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort/Barracuda) takes precedence over the backend it fronts because the gateway is what answers your RCPT.
key_findings.detail: Literal suffixes to match (case-insensitive, on the lowest-preference/all MX names): Microsoft 365 = *.mail.protection.outlook.com (also *.olc.protection.outlook.com); Google Workspace = aspmx.l.google.com / alt[1-4].aspmx.l.google.com / aspmx[2-3].googlemail.com; consumer Gmail = *.gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com; Proofpoint = *.pphosted.com and *.gslb.pphosted.com (Proofpoint on Demand), *.ppe-hosted.com (Proofpoint Essentials); Mimecast = *.mimecast.com / *.mimecast.co.za / *.mimecast-offshore.com; Cisco IronPort/Secure Email = *.iphmx.com (e.g. mx1.hc1983-11.iphmx.com); Barracuda = *.barracudanetworks.com incl. *.ess.barracudanetworks.com, and cudamail.com; Zoho = mx.zoho.com/mx2/mx3, mx.zoho.eu, mx.zoho.in; Amazon SES inbound = inbound-smtp.<region>.amazonaws.com; Yahoo/AOL = *.yahoodns.net (e.g. mta5.am0.yahoodns.net, mx.aol.com now routes here); Fastmail = *.messagingengine.com. All audit MX strings in domain_kb.json fit these exactly (amadeus-com.mail.protection.outlook.com, mxa-001a8401.gslb.pphosted.com, us-smtp-inbound-1.mimecast.com, mx1.hc5016-32.iphmx.com, aspmx.l.google.com). Precedence rule: if ANY MX matches a gateway suffix, classify as that gateway even when a backend MX (outlook/aspmx) is also present — audit confirms this: opengov.com lists both pphosted and aspmx.l.google.com and behaved as Proofpoint.

key_findings.claim: Microsoft 365 / Exchange Online: RCPT probing is USELESS — do NOT SMTP-verify. Both false-reject and unreliable-accept.
key_findings.detail: M365 with Directory-Based Edge Blocking (DBEB) returns 550 5.4.1 'Recipient address rejected: Access denied. AS(201806281)' as a POLICY block that also fires for many VALID external senders/mailboxes (anti-harvest + tenant policy), so a rejection is not a not-found signal. Conversely many tenants accept-all at RCPT and only bounce asynchronously. Audit ground truth: microsoft365 = 810 addresses, only 3 true 550 5.1.1 address_not_found vs 118 recipient_rejected (5.4.1) — i.e. the RCPT verdict is meaningless. Strategy: skip live RCPT probing for M365; rely on the pattern KB (first.last dominates, dot separator) + confidence scoring. Never interpret 5.4.1 as 'invalid'. Note the routing_loop bounces (cdk.com had 14) and dns_failure are transport-level, not mailbox signals.

key_findings.claim: Google Workspace, Proofpoint, Mimecast, and Cisco IronPort give TRUTHFUL RCPT results — probing WORKS (honest 550 5.1.1 for non-existent mailboxes).
key_findings.detail: These gateways perform recipient verification (LDAP/directory sync, call-ahead, or SMTP call-forward to the backend) so they reject unknown mailboxes at RCPT time with 550 5.1.1 address_not_found. Audit ground truth counts of genuine not-founds: google_workspace 175 (of 990), proofpoint 77 (of 715), cisco_ironport 52 (of 216), mimecast 15 (of 123) — all with near-zero policy false-rejects (proofpoint/mimecast recipient_rejected = 0). Trust: RCPT 250 = mailbox exists (subject to catch-all check), RCPT 550 5.1.1 / 5.1.10 = mailbox does not exist. Caveat: this holds only when the gateway has recipient verification ENABLED; if a Proofpoint/Mimecast instance fronts M365 with verification OFF it will accept-all and the real rejection happens later — detect this via a catch-all probe.

key_findings.claim: Yahoo/AOL and Amazon SES inbound are ACCEPT-ALL / ambiguous — RCPT gives false-accepts, treat as unreliable for mailbox-level verification.
key_findings.detail: Yahoo-family MX (*.yahoodns.net) uses deliberate anti-enumeration: 250 OK at RCPT does NOT mean the mailbox exists; expect 5-15% false positives. AOL now routes through the same infrastructure. Amazon SES inbound (inbound-smtp.<region>.amazonaws.com) accepts mail at the gateway and evaluates receipt rules AFTER acceptance, so RCPT-time it effectively accepts-all for the domain — no mailbox signal. For both: do not treat 250 as 'valid'; fall back to pattern/confidence. Consumer Gmail (gmail-smtp-in) does return honest 5.1.1 but applies heavy anti-abuse rate limiting from a single probing IP.

key_findings.claim: Zoho and Barracuda are conditionally reliable — probe but always run a catch-all check first.
key_findings.detail: Zoho (mx.zoho.com) generally returns honest 550 5.1.1 for unknown users; audit sample is tiny (2 domains, 2 addrs, 0 bounces) so confidence is low — treat as probeable-but-verify. Barracuda (*.barracudanetworks.com) recipient verification is configurable per deployment: when LDAP/Exchange recipient verification is on it rejects honestly at RCPT; when off it accepts-all and defers. So Barracuda cannot be trusted blindly — the catch-all probe determines whether its RCPT verdicts are meaningful.

key_findings.claim: Interpret SMTP response codes precisely and always run a catch-all probe before trusting any 250.
key_findings.detail: Code map the verifier should use: 250 = accepted (valid ONLY if domain is not catch-all); 550 5.1.1 / 5.1.10 / 'user unknown' / 'no such user' = true not-found (hard invalid, only trust on the reliable-provider set); 550 5.4.1 'Access denied' = M365 POLICY, NOT a mailbox signal (ignore for verdict); 5.7.x = policy/reputation block (not mailbox); 4xx (421/450/451 4.7.1 greylist, 452/552 rate limit) = transient, retry with backoff and rotate/warm IP, do not conclude. Catch-all detection: RCPT a random non-existent local part (e.g. no-such-user-<rand>@domain); if it returns 250, the domain accepts everything and per-mailbox RCPT is worthless — downgrade to pattern-confidence. This is needed on every provider including the reliable ones.

recommendations: Ship a provider->strategy table keyed by MX suffix. PROBE (trust RCPT verdicts): google_workspace (aspmx.l.google.com), proofpoint (pphosted.com/ppe-hosted.com), mimecast (mimecast.com), cisco_ironport (iphmx.com). NO_PROBE (rely on pattern KB + confidence, never SMTP-verify): microsoft365 (mail.protection.outlook.com). PROBE_WITH_CATCHALL_GUARD (verdict only if not catch-all and verification enabled): zoho, barracuda. NO_PROBE / accept-all (250 is meaningless): yahoo_aol (yahoodns.net), amazon_ses (inbound-smtp.*.amazonaws.com).

Classification order per domain: (1) resolve all MX; (2) if any MX matches a security-gateway suffix (Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort/Barracuda) classify as that gateway regardless of backend; (3) else match M365 / Google / Zoho / SES / Yahoo suffixes; (4) else 'other/unknown' -> default to PROBE_WITH_CATCHALL_GUARD at low confidence. Handle multi-MX/mixed setups (audit's navi.com mixes aspmx + inbound-smtp.us-east-1.amazonaws.com).

Verification pipeline for PROBE providers: MX up -> catch-all probe (random local) -> if catch-all, stop and return pattern-confidence; else RCPT the candidate -> 250 = valid, 550 5.1.1/5.1.10 = invalid, 4xx = retry (exp backoff, up to N) then unknown. Treat 5.4.1/5.7.x as non-signal.

For M365 domains, invest confidence in the pattern KB instead of SMTP: 77% first.last with dot separator globally, and audited M365 domains (amadeus, honeywell, capco, healthedge, entainindia) are ~first.last dominant — emit the pattern-derived guess with a 'not SMTP-verifiable' flag rather than a false invalid.

Operational hardening for probing: use warmed, rPTR-valid, SPF-aligned sending IPs; low per-IP RCPT rate with rotation; issue realistic EHLO/MAIL FROM; cache per-domain provider classification, catch-all status, and greylist behavior in domain_kb.json to avoid re-probing (extend the existing schema with provider, mx, catch_all: bool, rcpt_reliable: enum).

Persist a per-provider outcome log so the map self-corrects: track observed 5.1.1 vs 5.4.1 vs 250-then-bounce rates per provider (as the audit already did) and auto-flip a provider to NO_PROBE if its policy-reject rate crosses a threshold, mirroring the M365 finding.
caveats: Gateway reliability is deployment-dependent: Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort/Barracuda are only truthful when recipient verification is enabled. The audit shows the sampled deployments had it on, but a gateway fronting M365 with verification off will accept-all at RCPT and bounce later — the catch-all guard is the only runtime defense against assuming otherwise.

Zoho (2 domains/2 addrs, 0 bounces), Barracuda (not present in audit), Amazon SES and Yahoo/AOL are graded from web sources + training knowledge, not from this audit's data — lower confidence than the M365/Google/Proofpoint/IronPort/Mimecast verdicts which are audit-grounded.

MX suffix matching can be fooled by vanity/custom MX hostnames that CNAME to a provider; for unmatched MX, resolve the A record and WHOIS the IP before defaulting, and always fall back to catch-all-guarded probing at low confidence.
Provider behavior drifts over time (Google, Yahoo, and M365 periodically tighten anti-enumeration); the reliability map should be revalidated against live outcome telemetry rather than frozen.

Legal/ToS note: high-volume RCPT probing can violate provider ToS and trigger IP blocklisting; the strategy assumes rate-limited, warmed-IP probing only against the PROBE-classified providers, not blanket scanning.

The domain_kb.json read was truncated at line 1951 of 7681; MX patterns and provider classifications reviewed are representative and consistent across all sampled entries, but per-domain known-bad local parts beyond the first ~25 domains were not individually inspected (not needed for the provider->reliability mapping).

verification.dimension: Provider-specific behavior & MX classification for email verification

verified_findings.claim: MX-suffix classification map (M365 = *.mail.protection.outlook.com; Google = aspmx.l.google.com; Proofpoint = *.pphosted.com/*.ppe-hosted.com; Mimecast = *.mimecast.com; IronPort = *.iphmx.com; etc.) and all audit MX strings fit these exactly.
verified_findings.evidence: Verified directly against domain_kb.json (219 domains). Every cited literal matches: amadeus-com.mail.protection.outlook.com (M365), mxa-001a8401.gslb.pphosted.com (experian, proofpoint), us-smtp-inbound-1.mimecast.com (mimecast), mx1.hc5016-32.iphmx.com (ironport), aspmx.l.google.com (google). Proofpoint suffix families (pphosted.com, gslb.pphosted.com), Mimecast (*.mimecast.com incl. eu-/us-/usb- prefixes), IronPort (*.iphmx.com incl. regional .eu/.ca/.ap), and Zoho (mx.zoho.com/.in/.eu) all present. External MX-fingerprint references corroborate the suffix families.
verified_findings.correction: Minor: no Barracuda (*.barracudanetworks.com/cudamail), no Proofpoint Essentials (*.ppe-hosted.com), and no Fastmail (*.messagingengine.com) MX appear anywhere in the audit — those suffixes are from training/web knowledge, not audit-validated (finding's own caveats already note this for Barracuda).

verified_findings.claim: Security-gateway MX takes precedence over the backend it fronts; opengov.com lists both pphosted and aspmx.l.google.com yet behaves/classifies as Proofpoint.
verified_findings.evidence: domain_kb.json opengov.com mx = [mxb-009e4f01.gslb.pphosted.com, aspmx.l.google.com, mxa-009e4f01.gslb.pphosted.com], provider=proofpoint, 60 addrs, 0 bounces. Precedence rule holds in the data.

verified_findings.claim: Audit mixes navi.com aspmx + inbound-smtp.us-east-1.amazonaws.com (multi/mixed MX setups exist).
verified_findings.evidence: navi.com mx = [aspmx3.googlemail.com, inbound-smtp.us-east-1.amazonaws.com, alt2.aspmx.l.google.com], classified google_workspace. rapido.bike also mixes Google + inbound-smtp.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com. Confirms mixed-provider handling is needed.
verified_findings.correction: Note a classification-order ambiguity: navi/rapido were classified as Google (the primary/backend) despite carrying an SES-inbound MX. The recommended order '(2) gateway suffixes, (3) else match M365/Google/Zoho/SES/Yahoo' does not specify a Google-vs-SES tie-break; implement it as 'primary/lowest-preference backend wins over a secondary SES-inbound MX' to reproduce the audit's labeling.

verified_findings.claim: Microsoft 365 RCPT probing is USELESS: 550 5.4.1 'Access denied AS(201806281)' is a policy block (DBEB) that is not a mailbox-not-found signal; audit = 810 addrs, only 3 true 5.1.1 vs 118 recipient_rejected.
verified_findings.evidence: summary.md provider table matches (microsoft365: 810 addrs, 3 address_not_found, 118 recipient_rejected). Microsoft's own KB and Suped confirm 550 5.4.1 AS(201806281) is a DBEB edge rejection that fires when the address isn't in Entra ID BUT also for stale/unsynced directory data and tenant policy — so a prober cannot distinguish not-found from policy-block. Conclusion that 5.4.1 must never be read as 'invalid' is correct.
verified_findings.correction: Nuance, not a correction: the 3 genuine 5.1.1s show a minority of tenants (DBEB off / hybrid InternalRelay) do return honest not-founds, but they are indistinguishable from accept-all tenants at probe time, so 'skip RCPT, use pattern KB' remains the right strategy.

verified_findings.claim: Google Workspace, Proofpoint, Mimecast, Cisco IronPort return truthful 550 5.1.1 for non-existent mailboxes — RCPT probing works (with near-zero policy false-rejects).
verified_findings.evidence: summary.md: google_workspace 175/990 address_not_found, proofpoint 77/715 (recipient_rejected=0), cisco_ironport 52/216 (recipient_rejected=3), mimecast 15/123 (recipient_rejected=0). High honest-not-found rates and near-zero policy rejects confirm these gateways do recipient verification. Caveat that this only holds when recipient verification is ENABLED (a gateway fronting M365 with it off will accept-all) is technically correct and the catch-all guard is the right defense.

verified_findings.claim: Yahoo/AOL (*.yahoodns.net) and Amazon SES inbound (inbound-smtp.<region>.amazonaws.com) are accept-all/ambiguous — 250 at RCPT is meaningless, treat as NO_PROBE.
verified_findings.evidence: Yahoo: senders.yahooinc.com + multiple verification-vendor sources confirm Yahoo returns 250 to every RCPT as deliberate anti-harvesting; AOL shares Yahoo infra. SES: AWS docs confirm inbound receipt rules are evaluated AFTER SMTP acceptance and a rule without a recipient condition matches all recipients, so within a verified domain SES effectively accepts-all at RCPT. Audit shows yahoo.com under 'other' with yahoodns.net MX and navi/rapido carrying SES inbound MX.
verified_findings.correction: Refinement on SES: it is accept-all only for the LOCAL PART within a domain that is a verified SES identity/receiving domain; SES will reject at RCPT if the recipient domain is not configured for receiving. So 'accepts-all for the domain' is right for any domain actually routing to SES inbound (the only case that matters here).

verified_findings.claim: SMTP code map: treat 250 as valid only if not catch-all; 550 5.1.1/5.1.10 = not-found; 5.4.1 = M365 policy (ignore); 5.7.x = policy; 4xx (421/450/451 4.7.1 greylist, 452/552 rate limit) = transient retry.
