verified_findings.evidence: Code semantics are accurate per RFC 3463/5321 and provider docs: 5.1.1/5.1.10 = bad destination mailbox / recipient-not-found (5.1.10 is Microsoft's RESOLVER.ADR.RecipientNotFound / null-MX case), 451 4.7.1 is the canonical greylist code, 5.7.x = policy/security. Catch-all probe (random high-entropy local part; if 250 then downgrade to pattern-confidence) is standard and correct.
verified_findings.correction: One factual error: 552 is a 5xx PERMANENT code (RFC 5321: 'exceeded storage allocation'/'message too large'), not a 4xx transient — it is miscategorized under '4xx ... transient, retry.' The transient rate/quota code is 452 (and 421). Do not auto-retry a 552 as if transient; classify 552 as permanent (mailbox-full/size) and 452 as transient.

must_fix_errors: SMTP code map miscategorizes 552 as a '4xx transient, retry' code. 552 is a 5xx PERMANENT reply (RFC 5321: exceeded storage allocation / message too large). Retrying it as transient is wrong. Keep 421/450/451 4.7.1/452 as transient-retry; move 552 to the permanent set (mailbox-full/size-related, not a mailbox-existence signal).

dimension: Commercial finder/verifier APIs as pluggable fallbacks

research.dimension: Commercial email finder/verifier APIs as optional pluggable fallback providers (research for LinkedIn email finder + verifier)

key_findings.claim: Hunter.io has the cleanest, most implementation-friendly finder+verifier API pair, and uniquely returns pattern/source evidence you can feed back into your domain KB
key_findings.detail: FINDER: GET https://api.hunter.io/v2/email-finder with params domain|company|linkedin_handle plus first_name+last_name or full_name; optional max_duration (3-20s). Response: {email, score (0-100 confidence), verification:{date,status: valid|accept_all|unknown}, sources[], position, domain}. VERIFIER: GET https://api.hunter.io/v2/email-verifier?email=... Response: {status: valid|invalid|accept_all|webmail|disposable|unknown, score, and booleans regexp, gibberish, disposable, webmail, mx_records, smtp_server, smtp_check, accept_all, block, sources[]}; returns HTTP 202 if verification takes >20s (retry same request, counted once). AUTH: api_key query param, X-API-KEY header, or Authorization: Bearer. RATE LIMITS: finder 15 req/s and 500 req/min; verifier 10 req/s and 300 req/min. PRICING: credit charged only when an email is found; Free plan ~25 searches + 50 verifications/mo (migrated to Unified Credits July 2025: 1 search = 1 credit, 1 verification = 0.5 credit); Starter $49/mo ($34 annual) = 2,000 credits; Growth ~$104/mo annual; Scale ~$209/mo. Also has a domain-search endpoint that returns the domain's dominant email pattern — directly compatible with your domain_kb.json pattern field. Test key 'test-api-key' returns dummy responses for integration tests.

key_findings.claim: Anymail Finder is the best 'pay only for verified' finder fallback: 1 credit only when a verified email is returned, LinkedIn-URL input, catch-all resolution, no rate limits
key_findings.detail: FINDER: POST https://api.anymailfinder.com/v5.1/find-email/person, Authorization header (API key). Body: (full_name | first_name+last_name) + (domain | company_name), OR linkedin_url alone. Optional x-webhook-url header for async. Response: {credits_charged, email, email_status: valid|risky|not_found|blacklisted, valid_email (only when valid), mx_domain, mx_host, person_full_name, person_company_name, person_job_title}. Status codes: 400 bad input, 401 auth, 402 insufficient credits. Recommend 180s timeout (it does live SMTP + catch-all probing at request time). VERIFIER: POST /v5.1/verify-email {email} -> {email_status: valid|risky|invalid, mx_domain, mx_host}, flat 0.2 credit per verification. CHARGING: 1 credit per VALID find only — risky/not_found/blacklisted are free; repeat searches free within 30 days; 'Find Decision Maker' costs 2 credits; company search 1 credit for up to 20 emails. Advertises no daily API caps and auto-scaling. ACCURACY: 97%+ guarantee on 'verified' results with credit refund for bounces. PRICING: from ~$29/mo; 100 free trial credits (card verification required). This live-verification model directly addresses your #1 bounce cause (address_not_found from wrong guesses) — you never pay for a guess it couldn't verify.

key_findings.claim: MillionVerifier is the best-value standalone verifier API (~$3.70/1k, honest result taxonomy, 160 req/s), ideal as the cheap bulk-verification fallback
key_findings.detail: VERIFIER: GET https://api.millionverifier.com/api/v3/?api={key}&email={email}&timeout={2-60, default 20}. Response: {result: ok|invalid|catch_all|unknown|unverified|disposable, resultcode (numeric mirror), subresult, quality, email, didyoumean (typo suggestion), free (bool), role (bool), credits, executiontime, error, livemode}. Rate limit 160 req/s per account. Risky-classified results don't consume credits, BUT repeated verifications of the same email ARE charged each time — you must cache results yourself (unlike Anymailfinder/Prospeo which dedupe server-side). Bulk API at https://bulkapi.millionverifier.com (CSV upload + polling + report download). PRICING: pay-as-you-go, credits never expire: ~$37/10k ($0.0037/email), $129/100k, $449/1M; 100 free credits on signup (some sources say up to 500). Test key API_KEY_FOR_TEST returns random responses. Caveat matching your audit: like every RCPT-based verifier it returns catch_all (not valid/invalid) for accept-all domains and cannot give true answers behind M365's 5.4.1 policy wall — expect 'unknown'/'catch_all' there.

key_findings.claim: Findymail and Prospeo are strong runner-up finders, both LinkedIn-native with verified-only charging; Prospeo notably uses Bounceban-style catch-all verification
key_findings.detail: FINDYMAIL: base https://app.findymail.com, Authorization: Bearer {key}. POST /api/search/name {name, domain} -> {contact:{name, domain, email}}; POST /api/search/business-profile {linkedin_url} (full URL or username) -> same shape, 30 concurrent sync requests; POST /api/search/domain {domain, roles[max 3]}; POST /api/verify {email} -> status + provider info (1 verifier credit per attempt); optional webhook_url on finder endpoints for async; overall cap ~300 simultaneous requests. Pricing: $49/mo = 1,000 finder + 1,000 verifier credits, $99/5k, $249/25k; charged only for verified finds; credits roll over up to 2x monthly allowance; <5% bounce guarantee with refund; ranked #1 for accuracy/coverage in Clay's benchmark; typical find rate ~75% on large-list benchmarks. PROSPEO: POST https://api.prospeo.io/enrich-person, header X-KEY. Old /email-finder and /social-url-enrichment endpoints are DEPRECATED (removed March 2026) — use enrich-person. Body data object: (first/last or full_name)+(company_name|company_website|company_linkedin_url), OR linkedin_url alone, OR email; query flags only_verified_email, enrich_mobile. Response: {error, free_enrichment, person:{email:{status:'VERIFIED'|..., email, verification_method:'BOUNCEBAN', email_mx_provider}}, company}. Errors: NO_MATCH, INVALID_DATAPOINTS, INSUFFICIENT_CREDITS, INVALID_API_KEY (all HTTP 400), 429 rate limit. Charging: 1 credit per verified email, 10 credits for mobile, no charge on no-match, free re-enrichment within 90 days. Free tier 75 emails/mo; paid from ~$39/mo (~$0.01/email). The BOUNCEBAN verification_method means Prospeo pays for specialized catch-all/M365 resolution upstream — useful for exactly the domains where your own RCPT probe is blind.

key_findings.claim: Snov.io, Dropcontact, Apollo, and RocketReach are all poor fits as pluggable API fallbacks; Clearbit is effectively dead as a standalone API
key_findings.detail: SNOV.IO: OAuth2 client_credentials (client_id+secret -> 1h Bearer token), and finder/verifier are ASYNC two-step (POST .../start returns task_hash, then GET .../result) at 60 req/min — much more integration friction than one-shot REST; credits deducted only on successful results; ~$29-39/mo for 1,000 credits with API on entry tier. DROPCONTACT: GDPR-first (no stored database, real-time discovery), batch-oriented async API, but API access is gated to the Business tier (~€79/mo for 5k credits) and it's enrichment-oriented (send batch, poll for results) — fine for CRM cleaning, awkward as a per-lookup fallback; failed lookups re-credited. APOLLO: POST api/v1/people/match (X-Api-Key header, master key required for some endpoints) enriches from name/company/LinkedIn; 1 credit per email but +8 credits if a phone is returned (up to 9/record), credits tied to seat plans, API rate limits vary by plan, and emails are database-sourced (staleness risk) rather than live-verified — plus their ToS restricts standalone data resale. ROCKETREACH: annual plans ($399-$1,699/yr) with capped exports (1,200-20,000/yr) despite 'unlimited lookups'; API tied to plan exports; most expensive per contact; database-sourced. CLEARBIT/BREEZE: HubSpot acquired Clearbit (Nov 2023); Clearbit Connect shut down Dec 2024, free platform Apr 2025, Logo API Dec 2025; the person/company/reveal.clearbit.com Enrichment APIs are sunset/maintenance-mode and unavailable to new customers — enrichment now requires a HubSpot subscription (Breeze Intelligence, ~$45/mo for 100 credits). Do not build against it.

key_findings.claim: Verifier tier comparison: ZeroBounce is the premium option with the richest status taxonomy; Reoon is the budget option; NeverBounce/Emailable/Bouncer are mid-tier without a distinguishing feature for this project
key_findings.detail: ZEROBOUNCE: GET https://api.zerobounce.net/v2/validate?api_key=..&email=..&timeout=3-60 (also api-us/api-eu regional hosts). Response: {address, status: valid|invalid|catch-all|unknown|spamtrap|abuse|do_not_mail, sub_status (26 values incl. mailbox_not_found, antispam_system, greylisted, role_based, disposable, toxic, accept_all, possible_typo, alias_address), free_email, mx_found, mx_record, catchall_domain, did_you_mean, domain_age_days, smtp_provider, optional activity_data and verify_plus flags}. Rate limit effectively unbounded (80k requests/10s). Pricing: $39/2k PAYG (~$0.0195/email — 5x MillionVerifier), credits never expire, 100 free credits, ZeroBounce ONE $99/mo for 10k/mo. Its sub_status values map almost 1:1 to your audit's bounce reason_class taxonomy, and smtp_provider parallels your provider column. REOON: GET https://emailverifier.reoon.com/api/v1/verify?email=..&key=..&mode=quick|power. Quick (<0.5s, no inbox check): valid|invalid|disposable|spamtrap. Power: safe|invalid|disabled|disposable|inbox_full|catch_all|role_account|spamtrap|unknown + overall_score 0-100 + is_safe_to_send, mx_records; ~5 concurrent threads recommended; extremely cheap lifetime credits (from ~$11.90/10k per 2026 listings) plus daily-credit subscriptions from $9.95/500-day. NEVERBOUNCE (v4 single/check): ~$0.008/email PAYG falling to ~$0.004-0.005 at 100k+, but credits EXPIRE after 12 months — worst value of the group. EMAILABLE: ~$0.006/email at 10k tier, 250 free credits, response includes state/reason/score/smtp/catch-all/disposable flags, batches to 50k with callbacks. BOUNCER (usebouncer.com): $0.002-0.008/email, 500k for $1,000 never expiring, no charge for duplicates or unknowns. None of the verifiers can truly resolve M365 policy-blocked (5.4.1) mailboxes; for accept-all domains they all return a catch-all/risky class rather than a definitive answer.

key_findings.claim: RECOMMENDED PROVIDER SET (3): Anymailfinder as primary paid finder, Hunter.io as secondary finder + pattern-evidence source, MillionVerifier as the cheap verifier fallback
key_findings.detail: Rationale grounded in your audit: (1) Your own guesser (first.last 77% + domain KB) plus your own SMTP prober already covers Google Workspace/Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort domains (honest 550 5.1.1). The paid fallback is only needed for (a) M365 domains where RCPT probing is useless, (b) catch-all domains, (c) unknown-pattern domains. (2) Anymailfinder fits (a)+(b) best: it does live verification incl. catch-all resolution at request time, charges ONLY for verified results (so firing it exclusively at hard domains is cost-efficient), accepts linkedin_url directly (your product's input), has no rate caps, and offers a 0.2-credit verifier for the M365 addresses your prober can't judge. Findymail is a near-equal substitute here (same verified-only model, #1 in Clay's accuracy benchmark) — pick one, support both behind the same interface if trivial. (3) Hunter fits (c): its email-finder returns score + sources and its domain-search returns the domain's observed pattern, which you write back into domain_kb.json so the NEXT lookup on that domain is free. Generous free tier for development, test-api-key for CI. (4) MillionVerifier is the verification workhorse at $0.0037/email (5x cheaper than ZeroBounce) with honest ok/catch_all/unknown taxonomy at 160 req/s — use it to bulk-check your pattern guesses on domains where you don't want to run your own SMTP prober (e.g., from clean-IP concerns). Use ZeroBounce only if you later want its forensic sub_status taxonomy. Cost model for a typical hard lookup: own guess+probe $0 -> MillionVerifier check $0.0037 -> Anymailfinder verified find ~$0.02-0.15 depending on plan tier.

key_findings.claim: PROPOSED INTERFACE CONTRACT: two small interfaces (EmailFinder, EmailVerifier) with normalized result enums, provider-agnostic error taxonomy, cost accounting, and domain-KB feedback hooks
key_findings.detail: FINDER CONTRACT — input: FindRequest {first_name?, last_name?, full_name?, domain?, company_name?, linkedin_url?, timeout_ms?} (validate: linkedin_url alone OR name+company identifier; normalize name casing/diacritics before dispatch). Output: FindResult {email: string|null; status: FOUND_VERIFIED | FOUND_UNVERIFIED | FOUND_CATCH_ALL | NOT_FOUND; confidence: 0-100 (map: Hunter score as-is; Anymailfinder valid=95, risky=40; Findymail verified=95; Prospeo VERIFIED=95); pattern_hint?: e.g. 'first.last' (Hunter domain-search); provider: string; credits_charged: float; latency_ms; raw: provider JSON retained for audit}. Interface: name() string; find(ctx, FindRequest) -> (FindResult, error); estimated_cost_credits(req) float; healthy(ctx) bool (quota/auth ping via each provider's account endpoint). VERIFIER CONTRACT — verify(ctx, email, opts{timeout_ms, deep bool}) -> VerifyResult {email; status: DELIVERABLE | UNDELIVERABLE | RISKY | UNKNOWN; reason: mailbox_not_found | catch_all | policy_blocked | disabled | mailbox_full | greylisted | disposable | role_based | syntax | dns_failure | provider_timeout | other (this enum deliberately mirrors your audit's reason_class values); is_catch_all, is_disposable, is_role: bool; score: 0-100|null; mx_provider: string|null (normalize to your audit's provider taxonomy: microsoft365|google_workspace|proofpoint|mimecast|cisco_ironport|zoho|other); provider; credits_charged; raw}. STATUS MAPPING TABLE: MillionVerifier ok->DELIVERABLE, invalid->UNDELIVERABLE(mailbox_not_found), catch_all->RISKY(catch_all), disposable->RISKY(disposable), unknown/unverified->UNKNOWN; ZeroBounce valid->DELIVERABLE, invalid->UNDELIVERABLE(sub_status mapped), catch-all->RISKY(catch_all), spamtrap/abuse/do_not_mail->RISKY(other); Anymailfinder valid->DELIVERABLE, risky->RISKY, invalid->UNDELIVERABLE; Hunter valid->DELIVERABLE, accept_all->RISKY(catch_all), invalid->UNDELIVERABLE, unknown/webmail->UNKNOWN. ERROR TAXONOMY (typed errors, never stringly): ErrAuth (fail-fast, disable provider), ErrQuotaExhausted (skip provider until next billing tick), ErrRateLimited{retry_after} (backoff + honor per-provider limits: Hunter 15rps/10rps, MV 160rps, Findymail 30 concurrent, AMF unlimited but 180s timeout), ErrTimeout (retryable once), ErrProviderDown (circuit-break 5 min), ErrBadInput (non-retryable, caller bug). ORCHESTRATION POLICY (config, not code): chain = [local_pattern_guess -> local_smtp_probe (only if kb.provider in {google_workspace, proofpoint, mimecast, cisco_ironport}) -> verifier_fallback (MillionVerifier) -> finder_fallback (Anymailfinder -> Hunter)]; short-circuit on DELIVERABLE or FOUND_VERIFIED; on M365/catch-all domains skip local_smtp_probe entirely per audit ground truth. CACHING/IDEMPOTENCY: cache key sha256(provider + normalized_input), TTL 30d for verify results and 90d for finds (mirrors AMF 30-day / Prospeo 90-day free-repeat windows so a cache miss never double-charges); MillionVerifier has NO server-side dedupe so the cache is mandatory before it. FEEDBACK LOOP: every FOUND_VERIFIED result and every pattern_hint upserts domain_kb.json {pattern, provider, verified_examples+1}; every UNDELIVERABLE(mailbox_not_found) appends to known-bad local parts. CONFIG SHAPE: providers: [{name, api_key_env, enabled, max_credits_per_day, priority}] — providers are optional plugins; the system must run fully with zero providers configured (pure local mode).

recommendations: Support exactly 3 optional providers at launch: Anymailfinder (finder+verifier, POST /v5.1/find-email/person and /v5.1/verify-email, Authorization header) as primary paid finder because it charges only for verified results and resolves catch-alls live; Hunter.io (GET /v2/email-finder, /v2/email-verifier) as secondary finder chosen for its score/sources/pattern evidence that feeds domain_kb.json; MillionVerifier (GET api/v3) as the cheap bulk verifier at ~$0.0037/email. Treat Findymail as a drop-in alternative to Anymailfinder if its Clay-benchmark accuracy matters more than AMF's no-rate-limit scaling.

Gate paid calls with the audit-derived routing rule: never spend credits on domains where your own stack works — run local pattern-guess + SMTP probe for google_workspace/proofpoint/mimecast/cisco_ironport (honest 550 5.1.1), and route ONLY M365 (mx *.protection.outlook.com), catch-all, and unknown-pattern domains to commercial providers. This cuts paid volume by roughly the 60-70% of domains your audit shows are honestly probeable.

Implement the two-interface contract (EmailFinder, EmailVerifier) with the normalized status enums, typed error taxonomy (ErrAuth/ErrQuotaExhausted/ErrRateLimited{retry_after}/ErrTimeout/ErrProviderDown/ErrBadInput), per-provider rate limiting, circuit breakers, and a mandatory local result cache (30d verify / 90d find TTL) — MillionVerifier double-charges repeat verifications so cache-before-call is a hard requirement.

Wire the feedback loop: persist every provider raw response; upsert domain_kb.json with pattern/provider/verified examples from Hunter sources and any FOUND_VERIFIED result; append mailbox_not_found results to known-bad local parts. Providers should make your free path smarter over time, shrinking paid spend.

Ship providers as config-driven optional plugins ({name, api_key_env, enabled, priority, max_credits_per_day}) with the system fully functional in zero-provider local mode; use Hunter's test-api-key and MillionVerifier's API_KEY_FOR_TEST for CI contract tests without spending credits.

Do not build against Clearbit (sunset, HubSpot-only), Apollo (seat-tied credits, 9-credit worst case, DB-sourced staleness), RocketReach (export-capped annual plans), Snov.io (OAuth + async two-step friction), or Dropcontact (API gated to €79/mo Business tier, batch-oriented) — all are poor fits for a per-lookup fallback slot.
caveats: Pricing figures are July 2026 web-sourced and change frequently; free-tier numbers conflicted across sources (Hunter 25 searches vs 50 unified credits; MillionVerifier 100 vs 500 free credits; Reoon lifetime-credit floor) — re-verify on the pricing pages at implementation time.

Exact response JSON for Findymail /api/verify, NeverBounce v4, Emailable, and Bouncer was summarized from docs/search rather than captured verbatim; confirm field names against live sandbox calls before freezing the mapping table. MillionVerifier's numeric resultcode-to-result mapping was not captured verbatim either.

No commercial verifier truly penetrates M365 tenants that return blanket 550 5.4.1 — they will report unknown/risky/catch-all there. Only finder-side live-verification services (Anymailfinder, Findymail, Prospeo via Bounceban) claim partial catch-all resolution, and their 'verified' guarantee (97%+/<5% bounce with refund) is the practical ceiling; budget for a residual 2-5% bounce rate on M365/catch-all domains.
Prospeo's legacy /email-finder and /social-url-enrichment endpoints were deprecated with removal slated for March (docs did not state the year unambiguously); integrate only against /enrich-person.

Accuracy claims (AMF 97%+, Findymail Clay #1 / <5% bounce guarantee, Findymail ~75% find rate) are vendor or affiliate-adjacent sources, not independent audits; validate against a slice of your own 3,006-address ground-truth set before committing spend.

Scraping-adjacent LinkedIn enrichment (linkedin_url inputs) carries ToS/legal exposure that varies by provider and jurisdiction; this research did not cover case law — flag for separate legal review if LinkedIn-URL lookups are a core flow.

verification.dimension: Commercial email finder/verifier APIs as optional pluggable fallback providers — adversarial verification

verified_findings.claim: Hunter.io API pair: GET /v2/email-finder (domain|company|linkedin_handle + names, max_duration 3-20s, response with email/score/verification/sources) and GET /v2/email-verifier (status valid|invalid|accept_all|webmail|disposable|unknown + booleans, HTTP 202 on >20s); auth via api_key/X-API-KEY/Bearer; rate limits 15rps+500rpm finder, 10rps+300rpm verifier; 'test-api-key' dummy responses; domain-search returns the domain's email pattern; credit charged only when found
verified_findings.evidence: Live fetch of hunter.io/api-documentation/v2 confirmed every technical detail verbatim: max_duration 'must range between 3 and 20', 202-and-repoll behavior, all three auth methods, exact per-endpoint rate limits (15/500 finder, 10/300 verifier), test-api-key returning 'the same, dummy, response', and Domain Search's `pattern` field (e.g. '{first}'). Pricing page confirmed unified credits (1 credit = 1 search, 0.5 credit = 1 verification), 'If no emails are found, no credits are used', Starter $49/mo ($34 annual) = 2,000 credits, Growth $149 ($104 annual) = 10k, Scale $299 ($209 annual) = 25k, and same-email searches counted once per calendar month.
verified_findings.correction: One stale number: the Free plan is now 50 unified credits/month (= 50 searches or 100 verifications), not the legacy '25 searches + 50 verifications'. Everything else stands.

verified_findings.claim: Anymail Finder: POST https://api.anymailfinder.com/v5.1/find-email/person, Authorization-header key, name+domain/company or linkedin_url alone, x-webhook-url async, response {credits_charged, email, email_status valid|risky|not_found|blacklisted, valid_email, mx_domain, mx_host, person_*}; 400/401/402 errors; 180s recommended timeout; 1 credit only on valid find, risky/not_found/blacklisted free, 30-day free repeats; verify-email at 0.2 credit; no rate limits; 97%+ guarantee; ~$29/mo entry; 100 free trial credits with card
verified_findings.evidence: Live fetch of anymailfinder.com/email-finder-api/docs/find-person-email confirmed endpoint, auth, all body fields, x-webhook-url, exact response schema and email_status enum, 400/401/402, 180s timeout, 'No Rate Limits', and 'charged only when a valid email is found' with 30-day free repeats. docs/verify-email confirmed POST /v5.1/verify-email -> valid|risky|invalid at 0.2 credits with 30-day dedupe. Pricing page confirmed 0.2-credit verify, 2-credit decision-maker, 1 credit per up-to-20 company emails, '97%+ delivery guarantee', bounce re-credit via dashboard, and 100 free trial credits with card verification. Entry tier shown geo-localized at ₹2,599/mo for 400 credits (~$30), consistent with 'from ~$29/mo'.
verified_findings.correction: Minor additions: trial credits expire after 14 days; the pricing page qualifies full no-limit parallel throughput as a 100k+/mo-plan feature (docs still state no rate limits generally). Auth is a bare API key in the Authorization header (no Bearer prefix shown).

verified_findings.claim: MillionVerifier: GET https://api.millionverifier.com/api/v3/ (api, email, timeout 2-60 default 20), result ok|invalid|catch_all|unknown|unverified|disposable with resultcode/subresult/quality/didyoumean/free/role/credits/executiontime/livemode, 160 req/s, risky results free BUT repeat verifications charged every time (no server-side dedupe), bulkapi.millionverifier.com, ~$37/10k, $129/100k, $449/1M, credits never expire, 100 free credits, API_KEY_FOR_TEST
verified_findings.evidence: developer.millionverifier.com confirmed endpoint, params (timeout 2-60 default 20), full response field list, result values, API_KEY_FOR_TEST, and bulkapi host. help.millionverifier.com real-time-api page confirmed '160 requests per second' and 'If you verify the same email address multiple times, we will deduct the credits for all of the API calls' plus risky-not-charged — so the finding's cache-before-call requirement is correct and load-bearing. Homepage confirmed credits never expire, 100 free credits no card, $449/1M.
verified_findings.correction: Pricing is stale in the finder's favor: current published tiers show 50,000 for $89 ($0.00178/email) — cheaper than the claimed $0.0037; the $37 price point still exists but $129/100k was not confirmed (one 2026 third-party source even lists 1M at $549 vs the official $449 — trust the official page). The 'best-value verifier' conclusion is strengthened, but re-quote tiers at implementation time.

verified_findings.claim: Findymail: Bearer auth at app.findymail.com; POST /api/search/name, /api/search/business-profile (linkedin_url, 30 concurrent sync), /api/search/domain (roles max 3); POST /api/verify 1 credit per attempt; webhook_url async; ~300 simultaneous cap; verified-only charging; $49/mo=1k, $99/5k, $249/25k; 2x rollover; <5% bounce guarantee; Clay benchmark #1; ~75% find rate
verified_findings.evidence: Live docs fetch confirmed base URL, Bearer auth, all four endpoints with parameters, webhook_url, business-profile 'limited to 30 concurrent requests (when used synchronously)', domain search limited to 5 concurrent, global 'concurrent rate limit of 300 simultaneous requests', verifier 'uses one verifier credit on all attempted verification', and finder credits charged only on verified finds. Pricing page confirmed 2x rollover and '<5% bounce rate. If your bounce rate is higher, we refund your credits.'
verified_findings.correction: Two errors: (1) pricing is outdated — the current public Starter tier is $99/mo for 5,000 finder + 5,000 verifier credits; a $49/1k tier is no longer shown and $249/25k is unconfirmed (sliders show 15k/30k, custom quotes). (2) /api/verify returns {email, verified: true|false, provider} — a boolean, not a status enum; the mapping layer must treat it as binary. Clay-benchmark-#1 and ~75% find-rate remain unverified vendor/affiliate claims (already caveated).

verified_findings.claim: Prospeo: POST https://api.prospeo.io/enrich-person with X-KEY; inputs (names+company_* | linkedin_url | email); flags only_verified_email, enrich_mobile; response person.email {status VERIFIED, verification_method BOUNCEBAN, email_mx_provider}; errors NO_MATCH/INVALID_DATAPOINTS/INSUFFICIENT_CREDITS/INVALID_API_KEY + 429; 1 credit per verified email, 10 for mobile, free no-match, 90-day free re-enrichment; legacy /email-finder and /social-url-enrichment deprecated (removal March 2026)
verified_findings.evidence: Live fetch of prospeo.io/api-docs/enrich-person confirmed endpoint, X-KEY auth, all input combinations (plus person_id and only_verified_mobile not in the finding), both flags, email.status 'VERIFIED' with verification_method 'BOUNCEBAN' example, the error code set (plus INVALID_REQUEST and INTERNAL_ERROR), and charging exactly as claimed (1 credit verified email, 10 credits mobile with email included, no charge on no-match, 90-day free re-enrichment). Current docs no longer reference /email-finder or /social-url-enrichment at all — consistent with the deprecation/removal claim (today is July 2026, past the stated March 2026 removal). Free-tier 75/mo and ~$39/mo entry pricing were not re-verified.
verified_findings.correction: None material. Integrate only against /enrich-person, as recommended.

verified_findings.claim: Snov.io, Dropcontact, Apollo, RocketReach are poor per-lookup fallback fits; Clearbit is effectively dead standalone (HubSpot acquired Nov 2023; Connect shut Dec 2024, free platform Apr 2025, Logo API Dec 2025; enrichment requires HubSpot Breeze ~$45/mo per 100 credits)
verified_findings.evidence: Snov.io: live fetch confirmed OAuth2 client-credentials with expires_in 3600, async two-step task_hash pattern for finder/verifier, and 'API rate is limited to 60 requests per minute'. Apollo: docs.apollo.io/docs/api-pricing confirmed '1 credit for demographics/email; +8 credits if mobile phone is returned' (max 9/record). RocketReach: 2026 sources confirm annual plans with export caps of 1,200/3,600/20,000 per year and API gated to Ultimate (~$2,099/yr) — the 'export-capped despite unlimited lookups' characterization is accurate. Clearbit: multiple sources confirm the sunset cascade (Connect Dec 2024; free platform/Reveal Lite/TAM Apr 30, 2025; Logo API Dec 1, 2025), Breeze Intelligence ~$45/mo for 100 credits requiring a paid HubSpot subscription, and no standalone API for new customers. All four 'do not build against' verdicts stand.
verified_findings.correction: Three detail fixes: (1) Dropcontact — API (+MCP) access is included in the entry Starter plan at €79/mo, but that plan carries only 500 credits/mo, not 5k; the finding's tier name ('Business') and credit count are wrong, though the €79 API floor and batch/file-async orientation (real-time notifications are Enterprise-only) are right. (2) RocketReach current published prices are $329 (Essentials) / $829 (Pro) / $1,699 (Ultimate) per year — not '$399-$1,699' — and lookups are also capped (1,200-10,000/yr), not truly unlimited. (3) Snov.io charges 1 credit for results with 'valid' OR 'unknown' status — slightly worse than 'only on successful results'. One search summary misdates the acquisition to Dec 2024; the finding's Nov 2023 announcement date is correct.

verified_findings.claim: Verifier tier comparison: ZeroBounce premium (richest taxonomy, ~$0.0195/email, 80k req/10s), Reoon budget (quick|power modes), NeverBounce credits expire after 12 months, Emailable/Bouncer mid-tier
verified_findings.evidence: ZeroBounce live docs confirmed GET /v2/validate with timeout 3-60s, api-us/api-eu regional hosts, the 7 status values, sub_status values including mailbox_not_found/antispam_system/greylisted/role_based/disposable/toxic/accept_all/possible_typo/alias_address, response fields free_email/mx_found/mx_record/did_you_mean/domain_age_days/smtp_provider plus activity_data and verify_plus flags, and the '80,000 requests in 10 seconds' limit (100k for ONE customers); ZeroBounce ONE at $99/mo for 10k confirmed; $39/2,000-credit PAYG confirmed by multiple 2026 third-party sources; 'unknown' results are not charged. Reoon live docs confirmed emailverifier.reoon.com/api/v1/verify with mode quick|power, quick <0.5s without inbox check, the exact power-mode status list (safe/invalid/disabled/disposable/inbox_full/catch_all/role_account/spamtrap/unknown), overall_score 0-100, is_safe_to_send, and the ≤5-thread guidance. NeverBounce 12-month credit expiry and $0.008/email PAYG falling to ~$0.004-0.005 at 100k+ confirmed by multiple 2026 sources.
verified_findings.correction: Small fixes: ZeroBounce documents 24 sub_status values, not 26; its free credits are 100/month recurring (business-domain signups), not a one-time 100; and the official PAYG page now steers to a 10,000-credit minimum even though the $39/2k package is still widely quoted — verify at purchase. Emailable/Bouncer specifics were not independently re-verified (finding already carries a caveat); this doesn't affect the tiering conclusion.

verified_findings.claim: Recommended 3-provider set (Anymailfinder primary finder, Hunter secondary finder + pattern evidence, MillionVerifier cheap verifier) with audit-derived routing gating paid calls to M365/catch-all/unknown-pattern domains, cutting paid volume by ~60-70%
verified_findings.evidence: The routing math reconciles exactly with /Users/akashkumar/linkedin-email-finder/audit_analysis/summary.md: honestly-probeable providers (google_workspace 114 + proofpoint 25 + cisco_ironport 8 + mimecast 6 = 153 of 219 domains = 70%; 2,044 of 3,006 addresses = 68%) vs microsoft365's 118 recipient_rejected against only 3 true 5.1.1 not-founds. Each factual premise of the recommendation was independently confirmed above: AMF verified-only charging + linkedin_url input + live catch-all resolution + no rate caps; Hunter domain-search pattern field feeding domain_kb.json + test-api-key for CI; MillionVerifier price floor + 160 rps + honest catch_all/unknown taxonomy. The cost-model figure is now conservative (MV at $0.00178/email at 50k tier).
verified_findings.correction: None to the recommendation itself. Two notes: Findymail as the drop-in AMF alternative now starts at $99/mo (5k credits), changing the entry-cost comparison; and no commercial verifier resolves M365 5.4.1 policy blocks (the finding's own caveat), so the fallback chain should expect RISKY/UNKNOWN, not answers, on those tenants.

verified_findings.claim: Proposed EmailFinder/EmailVerifier interface contract: normalized enums, provider status-mapping table, typed error taxonomy with provider-specific limits (Hunter 15/10 rps, MV 160 rps, Findymail 30 concurrent, AMF unlimited/180s), 30d/90d cache TTLs mirroring AMF/Prospeo free-repeat windows, mandatory cache before MillionVerifier, domain-KB feedback loop, zero-provider local mode
verified_findings.evidence: All factual anchors verified against live docs: the four rate/concurrency limits are each confirmed verbatim; AMF 30-day and Prospeo 90-day free-repeat windows confirmed, so the 30d-verify/90d-find TTLs are correctly derived; MillionVerifier's charge-on-every-repeat behavior confirmed, making the mandatory-cache rule correct; the status-mapping rows for MillionVerifier, ZeroBounce, Anymailfinder, and Hunter use only status values confirmed to exist; the reason enum covers the audit's reason_class values (address_not_found->mailbox_not_found, recipient_rejected->policy_blocked, dns_failure, inactive_account->disabled) with sensible extensions.
verified_findings.correction: Three adjustments when freezing the contract: (1) Findymail's verifier returns a boolean `verified`, not a status enum — map true->DELIVERABLE, false->UNKNOWN/UNDELIVERABLE only with care (no reason granularity). (2) Hunter's 'webmail' status means the address is at a webmail provider (it may be perfectly deliverable), so UNKNOWN is acceptable but the mapper should also set a webmail flag rather than discard the signal. (3) Hunter also enforces 500/300 req-MINUTE ceilings alongside the per-second ones — the rate limiter needs both windows. Confidence constants (AMF risky=40, etc.) are unverifiable design choices, fine as defaults.

must_fix_errors: Findymail pricing is outdated: current public entry tier is $99/mo = 5,000 finder + 5,000 verifier credits; the claimed $49/mo=1k and $249/25k tiers are not on the current pricing page (findymail.com/pricing). Also its /api/verify returns {email, verified: bool, provider} — a boolean, not a status enum — so the proposed mapping table row for Findymail must be rewritten.
Hunter free plan is now 50 unified credits/month (50 searches or 100 verifications), not the legacy '25 searches + 50 verifications' (hunter.io/pricing).

MillionVerifier pricing numbers are stale (in the cheaper direction): official site now shows 50,000 credits for $89 = $0.00178/email and 1M for $449; the $129/100k figure is unconfirmed and one 2026 source lists 1M at $549 — re-quote at purchase. The 'best value' and 'cache mandatory' conclusions still hold.

Dropcontact detail is wrong: API (+MCP) access is included in the entry Starter plan at €79/mo, but that plan includes only 500 credits/mo — not 'Business tier' and not 5k credits. The poor-fit verdict (batch/file-async, real-time only on Enterprise) still stands.

RocketReach prices are $329/$829/$1,699 per year (not $399-$1,699), lookups are also capped (1,200-10,000/yr) alongside exports (1,200-20,000/yr), and API access is gated to Ultimate ~$2,099/yr — the finding understates how restricted it is.

ZeroBounce sub_status has 24 documented values, not 26; free credits are 100/MONTH recurring for business-domain signups (not one-time); official PAYG page now steers to a 10,000-credit minimum although $39/2k is still quoted by 2026 third-party sources.
Snov.io charges 1 credit for finder/verifier results with 'valid' OR 'unknown' status — not strictly 'only on successful results'; this makes it slightly worse than characterized (verdict unchanged).

research.dimension: Deliverability, bounce impact & sending limits (grounded in the 3,006-address / 654-message / 560-bounce cold-outreach audit)

key_findings.claim: The audit campaign ran at ~18.6% bounce rate — roughly 9x the 2% safe threshold and well past the >5% level where mailbox providers actively route the sender to spam
key_findings.detail: 560 bounced addresses out of 3,006 attempted (352 address_not_found + 134 recipient_rejected + 20 routing_loop + 17 dns_failure + 13 policy/spam + 10 inactive + 10 connection_failure + 3 group + 1 mixed). Industry consensus thresholds: <2% total bounce = acceptable (<0.5% excellent), 2–5% = warning zone where Gmail Postmaster reputation degrades and ISPs begin throttling, >5% = active spam-foldering and blocklist risk (e.g., Spamhaus). Bounce rate is tracked per sending DOMAIN and per IP, so damage persists across campaigns: every wrong first.last guess in this audit didn't just fail — it lowered inbox placement for every subsequent correctly-guessed address. This is the core business case for the verifier: hard bounces are one of the top three signals providers use for inbox-vs-spam decisions.

key_findings.claim: The audit's 'sender limit reached' error is Gmail's free-account cap: 500 recipients per ROLLING 24h, with behavioral enforcement that can fire far below 500 — and high bounce volume is itself a trigger
key_findings.detail: Free Gmail: max 500 recipients/rolling-24h (To+CC+BCC+replies all count; slots free up 24h after each send, not at midnight). Google Workspace paid accounts: 2,000/day. The 654 audit messages exceeded 500 outright, guaranteeing the 'You have reached a limit for sending mail. Your message was not sent.' block (lasts 1–24h). Critically, this error is a catch-all: Google's behavioral system suspends sending at much lower volumes when it sees burst sending, volume spikes, or — directly relevant here — 'a large number of un-delivering messages'. 560 bounces flowing back into the account reads to Google exactly like a compromised/spamming account; repeated episodes escalate from temporary block to account suspension. So the bounces and the sending-limit error the audit hit are causally linked, not independent problems.

key_findings.claim: Google/Yahoo bulk-sender rules (Feb 1, 2024, fully enforced since; Microsoft Outlook.com added similar rules in 2025) set hard compliance floors: SPF+DKIM+DMARC, spam rate <0.3%, one-click unsubscribe at 5,000+/day
key_findings.detail: ALL senders to Gmail (any volume): SPF or DKIM on the sending domain; valid forward AND reverse DNS (PTR) for sending IPs; TLS in transit; spam-complaint rate in Postmaster Tools below 0.3% (Google strongly advises staying under 0.1%). BULK senders (5,000+ msgs/day to Gmail, measured per primary domain): BOTH SPF and DKIM; DMARC record with minimum p=none; the From: header domain must ALIGN with SPF or DKIM domain; one-click unsubscribe via RFC 8058 List-Unsubscribe-Post headers in commercial mail (deadline was June 1, 2024) with unsubscribes honored within 2 days. Non-compliance now produces temporary rejections escalating to permanent 550s. Yahoo mirrors these. Even though a cold-outreach tool sending <5,000/day only legally needs the 'all senders' tier, aligned SPF+DKIM+DMARC is the de-facto floor because M365 tenants (the audit's #2 provider, 810 addresses) commonly hard-reject unauthenticated cold mail with 550 5.4.1 — some of the audit's 118 M365 'Access denied' rejections are plausibly reputation/auth-gated rather than pure directory-based policy.

key_findings.claim: SPF/DKIM/DMARC implementation spec: three DNS records, DKIM at 2048-bit, DMARC ramped p=none → quarantine → reject, all on a dedicated cold-outreach domain — never the primary company domain
key_findings.detail: SPF: TXT at domain root, e.g. 'v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all' (one SPF record only; ≤10 DNS lookups or it permerrors). DKIM: 2048-bit key recommended (1024 minimum for Gmail), published at selector._domainkey.domain, enabled in the Workspace Admin console so Google signs outbound mail. DMARC: TXT at _dmarc.domain, start 'v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@domain' to collect aggregate reports, move to p=quarantine then p=reject once reports show alignment. Alignment matters more than mere passing: the d= in DKIM or the SPF-checked envelope domain must match the From: domain (relaxed alignment allows subdomains). Buy a separate but similar domain for cold outreach (e.g. tryfrnd.app vs frnd.app) so the ~18% bounce disaster the audit created burns a sacrificial domain's reputation, not the corporate domain's; set up Google Postmaster Tools on it to watch domain reputation, spam rate, and delivery errors.

key_findings.claim: A provider-aware verify-before-send pipeline would have prevented ~348 of the audit's 560 bounces (62%) at zero cost, and an MX/DNS health pre-check another ~47 (8%) — together cutting the bounce rate from 18.6% to roughly 3%
key_findings.detail: Breakdown against audit ground truth: (1) address_not_found = 352 bounces, of which 348 were on providers the audit proved give honest 550 5.1.1 at RCPT time (Google Workspace 175, Proofpoint 77, Cisco IronPort 52, other 29, Mimecast 15) — a pre-send SMTP RCPT probe would have caught essentially all of these; only the 3 M365 not-founds are unreachable this way. (2) dns_failure 17 + connection_failure 10 + routing_loop 20 (14 at cdk.com alone): a pre-send check that MX records resolve and accept connections, plus a per-domain circuit-breaker after the first routing-loop bounce, catches most of these ~47. (3) The remaining hard core: 118 M365 recipient_rejected (550 5.4.1 policy — fires even for VALID mailboxes, so RCPT probing is useless there; the tool must classify M365 domains by MX *.protection.outlook.com and return 'unverifiable — pattern-confidence only'), 10 inactive_account, 13 policy/spam. Pipeline order: syntax → MX lookup → provider classification from MX → catch-all detection (probe a random local part like x7q2rt9@domain; if accepted, domain is catch-all and RCPT results are meaningless — note M365 tenants often accept-all by default, corroborating the audit) → RCPT probe only on honest-provider, non-catch-all domains → else fall back to the pattern KB (77% first.last prior, per-domain known-good/known-bad locals from domain_kb.json) with an explicit confidence score, and only send to sub-'verified' addresses at reduced volume or not at all.

key_findings.claim: Warmup is mandatory before any real volume: 2–4 weeks, starting at 10–20/day, never increasing more than ~20%/day, landing at a steady-state cold-send cap of ~30–50/inbox/day (75–100 absolute max)
key_findings.detail: Consensus ramp for a new domain/inbox: days 1–3: 10–15/day; days 4–7: 20–30/day; week 2: 40–70/day; weeks 3–4: 80–150/day only if metrics stay clean. Volume spikes (even 20→40 overnight) are the single most common cause of domain flagging; the audit's pattern — hundreds of messages in a burst from an unwarmed account — is the textbook trigger. Steady-state per-inbox cold volume: 30–50/day is the conservative recommendation, 75–100 the ceiling; scale horizontally with multiple inboxes across 2–3 sending domains (each warmed) rather than vertically. During and after warmup monitor: bounce <2–3%, spam complaints <0.1%, and keep genuine replies coming (reply rate >5–10% is both a campaign-quality and a deliverability signal, since providers weight positive engagement). Warmup is pointless without SPF/DKIM/DMARC already in place.

key_findings.claim: Safe cold-outreach cadence: throttled, human-paced sending with per-domain-recipient caps and immediate bounce-feedback loops
key_findings.detail: Operational cadence rules the audit violated and the tool should enforce: (a) randomized 1–3 minute gaps between sends, business-hours only in the recipient's timezone — never burst-blast 654 messages; (b) cap sends per RECIPIENT domain per day (e.g. ≤20–30 to amadeus.com/day rather than 209 addresses at once — gateways like Proofpoint/Mimecast rate-pattern inbound sources); (c) hard-stop circuit breaker: if bounces for a domain or the campaign exceed ~3–5%, pause automatically and re-verify the remainder (would have saved harman.com: 39 not-founds should have stopped sending after the first 3–4); (d) suppression list: every 550 5.1.1 local part is permanently banked as known-bad (domain_kb.json already does this) and never retried; (e) plain-text-leaning personalized messages, no link/image-heavy templates, real one-click opt-out even under 5k/day; (f) 2–3 follow-ups max with 3–4 day spacing, stopping on any reply/opt-out.

key_findings.claim: The 134 recipient_rejected bounces (118 on M365) were mostly not preventable by verification — they require reputation-side fixes and per-domain policy classification
key_findings.detail: M365's 550 5.4.1 'Access denied' comes from Directory-Based Edge Blocking / tenant anti-spam policy and fires before mailbox existence is consulted for external unauthenticated or low-reputation senders — the audit saw whole-domain rejection clusters (capco.com 24/51, entainindia.com 20/39, healthedge.com 14/48, aciworldwide.com 10/44) while other M365 tenants (amadeus.com 0/209, honeywell.com 0/58) accepted everything, proving it is tenant policy, not address validity. Implementation: classify these as 'policy-blocked domains' in the KB; the right responses are (1) full authentication + warmed domain to pass the reputation gate, (2) deprioritize or route these domains to a different channel (LinkedIn InMail), (3) never interpret 5.4.1 as 'address invalid' — the audit's data shows valid mailboxes get it too, so treating it as a hard-bounce-invalid signal would poison the KB.

recommendations: Build the verifier as a provider-gated pipeline: syntax check → MX resolution (reject/park on NXDOMAIN or unreachable MX: saves the audit's 27 dns/connection bounces) → provider classification from MX hostname (*.protection.outlook.com=M365, aspmx*.google.com=Workspace, *.pphosted.com=Proofpoint, *.iphmx.com=IronPort, *.mimecast.com=Mimecast) → catch-all probe with a random local part → SMTP RCPT probe ONLY on honest providers (Workspace/Proofpoint/IronPort/Mimecast) → pattern-KB scoring fallback for M365 and catch-alls. This alone would have prevented ~62% of the audit's bounces.

Return a typed verdict per address, not a boolean: {valid | invalid | catch_all | policy_blocked_m365 | unverifiable} plus a confidence score seeded from domain_kb.json (known-good locals list, dominant shape, 77% first.last prior). Only send to 'valid' by default; gate 'catch_all'/'unverifiable' behind an explicit low-volume risk mode.

Enforce sending compliance in the sender module: refuse to start a campaign unless the sending domain has SPF, DKIM (2048-bit), and DMARC (≥p=none) records verified via live DNS lookup, and warn that Gmail free accounts cap at 500 recipients/rolling-24h (Workspace: 2,000) — the audit's 654-message run guaranteed the 'sender limit reached' block it hit.

Use a dedicated, warmed sending domain (cousin of the primary domain), enroll it in Google Postmaster Tools, and implement a warmup scheduler: 10–15/day → +≤20%/day → steady state 30–50 cold sends/inbox/day; scale with more inboxes/domains, not higher per-inbox volume.

Implement runtime circuit breakers: pause the campaign automatically when rolling bounce rate exceeds 3% (hard stop at 5%), pause a recipient domain after 2 consecutive not-found bounces or any routing-loop bounce, and randomize 1–3 min inter-send delays with per-recipient-domain daily caps (~20–30).

Close the feedback loop: parse every DSN, classify by enhanced status code (5.1.1=invalid → permanent suppression + KB known_bad_locals update; 5.4.1 on M365=policy → mark domain policy_blocked, do NOT mark address invalid; 4.x.x=soft → retry ≤2 times over 48h), so the KB gets smarter with every send exactly as domain_kb.json was built.

Even below the 5,000/day bulk threshold, ship one-click unsubscribe (RFC 8058 headers) and keep spam-complaint tracking against the 0.3% hard / 0.1% target thresholds — cold outreach lives or dies on staying under Google's and Yahoo's 2024+ enforcement lines.
caveats: Warmup ramp numbers and per-inbox caps (30–50/day, ≤20% daily increase) come from practitioner/vendor blogs, not provider documentation — Google never publishes behavioral-enforcement thresholds, so treat them as heuristics with safety margin, not guarantees.

The Gmail 500/day figure is for free consumer accounts and counts recipients (not messages); Google Workspace is 2,000/day. Google can and does block below these caps on behavioral signals, so the cap should be treated as a ceiling, not a budget.

M365 550 5.4.1 behavior is per-tenant policy: the audit shows some tenants (amadeus.com) accept everything while others (capco.com) reject en masse — so 'M365 = unverifiable' is the safe default, but per-tenant behavior can drift and should be re-learned from live DSNs.
Catch-all status is dynamic (admins toggle it); a catch-all verdict should carry a TTL (~30 days) rather than being cached forever.

The 5,000/day bulk-sender threshold is measured by Google per primary domain including subdomains, across all mail streams — a growing product can cross it unexpectedly; some 2025+ reporting indicates enforcement has tightened to effectively require DMARC at lower volumes.

Unsolicited SMTP RCPT probing at scale from a single IP can itself get the probing IP greylisted/blocklisted (especially by Proofpoint/IronPort); production verifiers rotate IPs, cache results, and throttle probes per MX — budget for this in architecture.

Several cited web sources are dated 2025–2026 and blog-grade; the authoritative baselines are Google's 'Email sender guidelines' (support.google.com/a/answer/81126), Yahoo Sender Hub, RFC 7208 (SPF), RFC 6376 (DKIM), RFC 7489 (DMARC), RFC 8058 (one-click unsubscribe).

verification.dimension: Deliverability, bounce impact & sending limits (adversarial verification of prior agent's findings against audit ground truth + web sources)

verified_findings.claim: Audit ran at ~18.6% bounce rate (560/3,006), ~9x the 2% safe threshold; >5% triggers spam-foldering
verified_findings.evidence: Bounce-class sum in summary.md is exactly 560 (352+134+20+17+13+10+10+3+1); 560/3006 = 18.63%. records.csv confirms 559 'Bounced' + 1 forwarding-failure = 560. The 2%/5% thresholds match broad practitioner consensus.
verified_findings.correction: Two soft corrections: (a) records.csv contains 32 additional 'Ambiguous campaign-level bounce' rows excluded from the 560, so 18.6% is a floor (worst case ~19.7%); (b) Gmail Postmaster Tools does NOT display bounce rate (it shows spam rate, IP/domain reputation, auth) — bounce thresholds (2%/5%) are industry heuristics, not published provider limits, and 'top three signals' is unverifiable vendor framing. Directionally sound; don't present the thresholds as provider-documented.

verified_findings.claim: 'Sender limit reached' is Gmail free-account 500-recipients/rolling-24h cap; Workspace = 2,000/day; high bounce volume itself triggers blocks below the cap
verified_findings.evidence: Google documents free Gmail at 500 recipients/day with 1–24h recovery (support.google.com/mail/answer/22839) and Workspace at 2,000 messages/24h, 3,000 unique / 10,000 total recipients (support.google.com/a/answer/166852). Rolling-24h behavior and behavioral enforcement below the cap (incl. 'large number of undeliverable messages') match Google's documentation and consistent third-party reporting.
verified_findings.correction: Minor: the limit counts RECIPIENTS, so the binding number is the 3,006 attempted recipients (~6x the cap), not the 654 messages — though 654 messages > 500 exceeds it either way. 'Guaranteed the block' assumes the sends occurred within one 24h window, which the audit files don't record — very likely but not provable from the data. Rolling-24h slot-freeing detail is community-reported, not in Google's official doc.

verified_findings.claim: Google/Yahoo Feb-2024 rules (+ Microsoft 2025): SPF+DKIM+DMARC, spam rate <0.3%, RFC 8058 one-click unsubscribe at 5,000+/day
verified_findings.evidence: All specifics verified against support.google.com/mail/answer/81126 and coverage: all-sender tier (SPF or DKIM, FCrDNS/PTR, TLS, spam rate <0.3% with <0.1% target), bulk tier at 5,000+/day per primary domain (both SPF and DKIM, DMARC ≥p=none, From-domain alignment, RFC 8058 one-click unsub deadline June 1 2024, 2-day honor window). Microsoft's requirements for 5,000+/day senders to Outlook.com took effect May 5, 2025, rejecting with '550 5.7.515' (techcommunity.microsoft.com).
verified_findings.correction: One embedded sub-claim is wrong: 'some of the audit's 118 M365 5.4.1 rejections are plausibly reputation/auth-gated' — Microsoft's auth/reputation rejections use 5.7.x codes (5.7.1, 5.7.515, 5.7.606); 550 5.4.1 'Recipient address rejected: Access denied' is specifically Directory-Based Edge Blocking (recipient not found in the tenant directory), per Microsoft's own NDR documentation. The 118 are directory verdicts, not auth verdicts.

verified_findings.claim: SPF/DKIM/DMARC implementation spec (single SPF record, ≤10 lookups, DKIM 2048-bit/1024 min, DMARC p=none→quarantine→reject, relaxed alignment, dedicated cousin sending domain)
verified_findings.evidence: All details check out: RFC 7208 §4.6.4 10-DNS-lookup permerror limit and one-SPF-record rule; Google requires ≥1024-bit DKIM and recommends 2048; DMARC at _dmarc.<domain> with p=none + rua ramping to quarantine/reject; relaxed alignment permits subdomains (RFC 7489); sacrificial cousin domain + Postmaster Tools is standard cold-outreach practice.

verified_findings.claim: Verify-before-send would have prevented ~348 bounces (62%) at zero cost, MX/DNS pre-check another ~47 (8%), cutting bounce rate from 18.6% to roughly 3%
verified_findings.evidence: The counts are right: per-provider address_not_found in summary.md is exactly Google 175 + Proofpoint 77 + IronPort 52 + other 29 + Mimecast 15 = 348 (62.1%), and dns 17 + connection 10 + routing_loop 20 = 47 (domain_kb.json confirms 14 routing_loops at cdk.com). But the headline conclusion fails twice. (1) Arithmetic: 560 − 348 − 47 = 165 residual bounces → 165/3006 = 5.5% (or 165/2611 = 6.3% if flagged addresses are withheld) — NOT ~3%; ~1.9% is reachable only by ALSO suppressing the 118 M365 policy-blocked addresses, an intervention the claim explicitly separates out. (2) 'Zero cost' contradicts the repo's own environment_constraints.md: outbound port 25 is BLOCKED on the dev machine and typically on residential ISPs, so pre-send RCPT probing requires a VPS with open port 25 or a paid HTTPS verifier API (ZeroBounce/Reoon) — real infra/dollar cost, and probe IPs risk greylisting by Proofpoint/IronPort (their own caveat).
verified_findings.correction: Restate as: RCPT verification on honest providers prevents ~348/560 (62%) and MX/health checks ~47 (8%), leaving a residual bounce rate of ~5.5–6.5%; getting to ≤2% additionally requires withholding or down-weighting M365 policy-blocked/unverifiable addresses. Replace 'zero cost' with 'no per-lookup vendor fee, but requires port-25-capable infrastructure or a paid verifier API' — this is a mandatory architecture constraint per environment_constraints.md. Also note the stated pipeline (probe only Workspace/Proofpoint/IronPort/Mimecast) excludes the 29 'other'-provider bounces it counts as prevented — 319 (57%) under its own rules.

verified_findings.claim: Warmup mandatory: 2–4 weeks, 10–20/day start, ≤20%/day increase, steady state 30–50/inbox/day (75–100 max)
verified_findings.evidence: Numbers match the practitioner/vendor consensus range (Mailreach, Smartlead, etc.) and the finding correctly self-labels them medium-confidence heuristics; no provider publishes behavioral thresholds, so they cannot be confirmed or refuted authoritatively.
verified_findings.correction: Keep, but present as safety heuristics with margin, exactly as the existing caveat says — never as provider-documented limits.

verified_findings.claim: Safe cadence: randomized 1–3 min gaps, per-recipient-domain caps ~20–30/day, 3–5% circuit breaker, permanent 5.1.1 suppression, 2–3 follow-ups
verified_findings.evidence: Operational heuristics, internally consistent with the audit data (harman.com's 39 not-founds out of 104 confirmed in summary.md — a circuit breaker after 3–4 consecutive not-founds would have saved ~35). Suppression of 5.1.1 locals matches how domain_kb.json known_bad_locals is built. Specific numbers (1–3 min, 20–30/domain) are vendor-blog folklore with no authoritative source.
verified_findings.correction: Sound engineering defaults; mark the specific numbers as tunable heuristics. Note per-recipient-domain caps matter most for gateway providers (Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort) that rate-pattern inbound sources.

verified_findings.claim: The 134 recipient_rejected (118 M365 550 5.4.1) were mostly NOT preventable by verification — 5.4.1 is tenant policy that fires even for valid mailboxes, so never interpret it as address-invalid
verified_findings.evidence: The counts and clusters are all accurate (capco 24/51, entainindia 20/39, healthedge 14/48, aciworldwide 10/44, amadeus 0/209, honeywell 0/58 — all match summary.md; 118+10+3+3=134). But the mechanism is inverted: Microsoft's own NDR documentation (support.microsoft.com, NDR 550 5.4.1) defines 'Recipient address rejected: Access denied' as Directory-Based Edge Blocking — the message is rejected because NO matching recipient object exists in the tenant's Entra ID/O365 directory. It fires because the directory was consulted, not before it. The audit's cluster pattern (partial rejection within a domain, remainder delivered) is exactly the DBEB signature of wrong first.last guesses, NOT proof that valid mailboxes were rejected. Valid mailboxes can hit 5.4.1 only via directory-sync lag or authoritative/internal-relay misconfiguration — whole-domain-breaking edge cases implausible for functioning companies like Capco.
verified_findings.correction: Correct interpretation: on DBEB-enabled M365 tenants, 550 5.4.1 is a STRONG probable-invalid signal (Microsoft's equivalent of user-unknown), so most of the 118 were preventable wrong guesses — raising total preventable bounces to ~466/560 (~83%). Discarding 5.4.1 as 'policy, not invalid' would throw away 118 true negatives and poison the KB in the opposite direction claimed. Practical rule: per-tenant classification via a random-local-part probe — if the random local gets 5.4.1, DBEB is on and 5.4.1 verdicts are meaningful; if it is accepted, the tenant is accept-all (like amadeus.com plausibly is) and RCPT results are meaningless. Also fix the related sub-claim 'M365 tenants often accept-all by default': the default for authoritative accepted domains in Exchange Online is DBEB ON; accept-all arises from internal-relay config or a third-party gateway in front. Caveat retained: DSN-time 5.4.1 (post-send) is reliable; pre-send RCPT probing of M365 edges is still operationally unreliable (EOP throttles/tarpits probing IPs), so the audit's 'RCPT probing is useless on M365' remains a fair engineering default even though the 5.4.1 semantics were misread.

verified_findings.claim: cdk.com accounted for 14 of the 20 routing_loop bounces
verified_findings.evidence: domain_kb.json entry for cdk.com: reason_classes {"routing_loop": 14}, provider microsoft365, MX cdk-com.mail.protection.outlook.com.

must_fix_errors: Claim 5's headline '18.6% → roughly 3%' is arithmetically wrong: the two named interventions leave 165 residual bounces → ~5.5–6.3% bounce rate; ~2–3% is reachable only by also suppressing the 118 M365 policy-blocked addresses. Restate the residual honestly or add the third intervention explicitly.

Claim 5's 'zero cost' is false in this project's own environment: environment_constraints.md documents that outbound port 25 is blocked on the dev machine (and typically on residential ISPs), so SMTP RCPT verification requires port-25-capable infrastructure (VPS) or a paid HTTPS verifier API behind a pluggable interface, and must degrade gracefully to 'verification_unavailable'. This is a hard architecture constraint the findings ignore.

Claim 8's core mechanism is inverted: per Microsoft's NDR documentation, 550 5.4.1 'Recipient address rejected: Access denied' IS Directory-Based Edge Blocking — recipient not found in the tenant directory — i.e., mostly a true invalid-address signal, not a policy block on valid mailboxes. The recommendation 'never interpret 5.4.1 as address invalid' would discard ~118 true negatives and poison the KB. Correct rule: treat post-send 5.4.1 on DBEB tenants as probable-invalid; classify tenants (DBEB vs accept-all) with a random-local-part probe; keep 'pre-send RCPT probing M365 is unreliable' as the operational default.

Claim 3's aside that some 118 M365 5.4.1s are 'plausibly reputation/auth-gated' is wrong — Microsoft auth/reputation rejections use 5.7.x codes (5.7.1/5.7.515/5.7.606), never 5.4.1. Remove or recode this.

Claim 5/8 sub-claim 'M365 tenants often accept-all by default' is backwards: authoritative accepted domains have DBEB enabled by default; accept-all behavior comes from internal-relay configuration or third-party gateways in front of EOP.

dimension: Legality & ethics: email finding, LinkedIn, cold outreach

research.dimension: Legality & ethics: email finding, LinkedIn data, and cold outreach (GDPR, CAN-SPAM, CASL, DPDP, CCPA, LinkedIn ToS/litigation) — with concrete design constraints for a MailMeteor-style finder/verifier

key_findings.claim: Deriving an email from name+company-domain is legally very different from scraping LinkedIn — but the OUTPUT is still personal data under GDPR either way
key_findings.detail: Permutation-based derivation (guess first.last@domain from a name the user already has, then verify DNS/SMTP) touches no LinkedIn systems, breaches no contract with LinkedIn, and implicates no CFAA/ToS issue. However, a work email tied to an identifiable person IS personal data under GDPR Art. 4(1) (and now under CCPA and India's DPDP Act), so generating/verifying/storing it is 'processing' requiring a lawful basis, transparency, retention limits, and erasure rights. The legal risk concentrates almost entirely on (a) HOW the name/company was obtained (scraping vs manual entry) and (b) what the SENDING complies with — not on the string-permutation act itself. This is exactly the architecture split the tool should exploit: manual/CSV name+company input in, derived+verified email out.

key_findings.claim: hiQ v. LinkedIn ended with hiQ LOSING: scraping public LinkedIn profiles is likely not a CFAA crime, but it IS a breach of LinkedIn's User Agreement — the case does NOT bless scraping
key_findings.detail: The 9th Circuit (2022, post-Van Buren remand) held that scraping publicly available pages likely does not violate the CFAA ('gates-up' theory). But on remand, in Nov 2022 Judge Chen granted LinkedIn summary judgment that hiQ breached the User Agreement via automated scraping and fake accounts; the Dec 2022 consent judgment had hiQ stop all scraping, delete all scraped data and derived algorithms, and pay $500,000. Net rule of thumb: public scraping ≈ not-a-federal-crime, still-a-contract-breach, still-account-termination, still-injunction-and-damages exposure. Widespread claims that 'hiQ made LinkedIn scraping legal' are wrong.

key_findings.claim: Meta v. Bright Data (N.D. Cal., Jan 2024) protects only LOGGED-OFF scraping of public pages; LinkedIn's live enforcement posture (ProAPIs suit, 2025) targets logged-in/fake-account scraping hard
key_findings.detail: The Bright Data summary judgment held Meta's ToS govern 'your use' of the service, so logged-off scraping of public data by a non-user did not breach the contract. That logic is narrow: the moment scraping happens through an account (which almost all LinkedIn email-finder extensions require, since emails/full profiles sit behind the login wall), the User Agreement binds and the hiQ precedent applies. LinkedIn sued ProAPIs (Oct 2025) over ~1M fake accounts scraping member data sold for up to $15k/month, seeking injunction, deletion, and damages; the parties reached an agreement in principle to settle (2026). LinkedIn UA §8.2 explicitly prohibits developing, supporting, or USING 'software, devices, scripts, robots... (including crawlers, browser plugins and add-ons)' to scrape the Services — meaning a tool VENDOR that automates LinkedIn violates the UA even if only its users hold accounts, and its users risk permanent bans.

key_findings.claim: The Kaspr fine (CNIL, Dec 2024, €240,000) is the controlling GDPR precedent for LinkedIn-based email finders — it defines exactly what regulators punish
key_findings.detail: Kaspr (a Chrome-extension email finder with ~160M contacts scraped from LinkedIn) was fined for: (1) collecting contact details of users who had restricted profile visibility — no valid legitimate interest because it defeated users' expressed choices; (2) 5-year rolling retention that effectively never expired; (3) no Art. 14 notice to the people in its database (and later only in English); (4) answering access requests with just 'publicly available sources' instead of the actual source. The lessons for tool design: honor platform visibility settings, hard retention caps, notify or be able to notify data subjects, and log provenance per record so access requests can be answered specifically. A derivation-only tool that stores nothing (or only the user's own prospect list) avoids nearly all of this exposure because it never builds a scraped database of third parties.

key_findings.claim: GDPR: B2B cold email can rest on legitimate interest (Recital 47), but only with a documented LIA, Art. 14 notice within one month, easy opt-out — and ePrivacy national law can still forbid it (Germany requires opt-in)
key_findings.detail: Recital 47 says direct marketing 'may' be a legitimate interest; the EDPB's Guidelines 1/2024 on legitimate interest stress it is never automatic — you need the three-part test (purpose/necessity/balancing) recorded in a Legitimate Interest Assessment (ICO publishes a free LIA template). Because the address is obtained from a third party/derived rather than from the data subject, Art. 14 requires informing the person within 1 month or at first communication — in practice the first email must disclose who you are, why you're writing, where you got their details, and how to object (Art. 21 objection to direct marketing is absolute — must stop, no balancing). Critically, GDPR is not the whole story: the ePrivacy Directive (Art. 13) governs unsolicited email per-country; Germany (UWG §7) treats B2B cold email without prior express consent as unlawful, while other member states are permissive for corporate addresses. A compliant tool must therefore support per-country sending policies, suppression lists, and objection handling.

key_findings.claim: CAN-SPAM (US): opt-out regime, no consent needed, but strict per-message formalities — up to $53,088 per email (2025 inflation-adjusted), B2B included
key_findings.detail: CAN-SPAM permits unsolicited commercial email but requires: truthful headers/From, non-deceptive subject line, identification as an ad (contextually), a valid physical postal address, a clear opt-out honored within 10 business days, and no sending to opted-out addresses. It makes no B2B/B2C distinction. Penalties run to $53,088 per separate violating email (FTC adjustment effective Jan 17, 2025, updated annually); the FTC's 2024 Verkada settlement ($2.95M, largest CAN-SPAM penalty to date) turned on ignored opt-outs and missing postal addresses — i.e., the mechanical stuff a tool can enforce. Notably, harvesting addresses and dictionary attacks (exactly what unrestrained permutation-plus-probe at scale looks like) are aggravated violations under 15 U.S.C. §7704(b), which is a reason to keep verification volumes low and user-initiated.

key_findings.claim: CASL (Canada) is the strictest major regime: opt-in by default, with a narrow 'conspicuously published' implied-consent path that fits role-relevant B2B outreach; sender bears burden of proof
key_findings.detail: Sending a commercial electronic message to a Canadian requires express or implied consent. The implied-consent route most relevant to an email finder: the address was conspicuously published (e.g., company website), with no accompanying no-unsolicited-email statement, AND the message is relevant to the recipient's business role/functions — all three must hold, and the SENDER must be able to prove it. A GUESSED address (permutation-derived, never published anywhere) does not satisfy 'conspicuously published', so pure derivation output has no implied-consent basis for Canadian recipients — a real gap the tool should surface. Every CEM also needs sender identification, mailing address, and an unsubscribe honored within 10 business days. Penalties: up to CAD $10M per violation (org) / $1M (individual).

key_findings.claim: India DPDP Act 2023: Rules notified 13 Nov 2025 with phased enforcement — full consent/notice/rights compliance bites 14 May 2027; consent-centric with NO legitimate-interest basis
key_findings.detail: The DPDP Rules 2025 (notified 13 Nov 2025) phase in over 18 months: Data Protection Board immediately, consent-manager registration at 12 months (14 Nov 2026), and the substantive consent/notice/data-principal-rights/grievance provisions at 18 months (14 May 2027). Unlike GDPR, DPDP has no legitimate-interest ground — lawful processing requires consent or narrow 'certain legitimate uses' (§7), and cold marketing isn't among them; notices must be standalone and plain-language, and consent withdrawable. Since the audit data shows heavy .in/India-office targeting (easebuzz.in, navi.com, wingify.com etc.), this matters: after May 2027 a commercial email-finding service processing Indian residents' data digitally will need a consent story or fall outside scope only if the processing is 'personal or domestic' (individual job-seeker outreach plausibly qualifies under §3's personal-purpose exclusion). Penalties up to ₹250 crore per breach class.

key_findings.claim: CCPA/CPRA: the B2B exemption died Jan 1, 2023 — California work emails/titles are protected personal information, and a contact-selling service can be a registrable 'data broker' under the Delete Act
key_findings.detail: Since the B2B/employee exemptions expired, California residents' business-contact data (work email, direct dial, title) carries full CCPA rights: know, correct, delete, opt out of sale/sharing. CCPA applies only to businesses over thresholds (>$25M revenue, OR data on ≥100k consumers/households, OR ≥50% revenue from selling personal info) — a small tool is likely exempt, but the 100k-records threshold is easy to cross if the tool retains a shared contact database. The Delete Act (SB 362) additionally requires any business that collects and SELLS personal info of consumers it has no direct relationship with to register as a data broker and honor the centralized DROP deletion mechanism — a strong argument for the tool to never resell or pool contacts across users.

key_findings.claim: SMTP verification itself has an ethics/abuse dimension the audit data sharpens: probe only where it's informative, keep volumes human-scale, and never 'verify by sending'
key_findings.detail: RCPT-TO probing without sending mail is not clearly regulated anywhere, but at scale it resembles a CAN-SPAM §7704(b)(1) 'dictionary attack' pattern, triggers greylisting/blocklisting (Spamhaus treats verification services as abuse sources), and burns the user's IP/domain reputation. The audit ground truth makes restraint cheap: on Microsoft 365 (46 domains, 118 policy-blocked 5.4.1s vs 3 true 5.1.1s) probing yields ~zero signal, so the tool should skip RCPT entirely and rely on pattern priors (77% first.last) + domain KB; on Google Workspace/Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort honest 5.1.1s make ONE targeted probe per candidate defensible. Cap probes per domain per day, randomize timing, use a dedicated probing IP, and never fall back to sending a 'test' email — that IS the unsolicited email regulators care about.

key_findings.claim: A job-seeker's individual referral outreach sits in the most-protected corner of every regime: likely not 'commercial' under CAN-SPAM, plausibly 'purely personal' under GDPR/DPDP — but etiquette and platform rules still apply
key_findings.detail: CAN-SPAM covers messages whose PRIMARY PURPOSE is commercial advertisement/promotion of a product or service; an individual asking about a job or referral is generally not advertising a product, so the Act's formalities (postal address, ad identification) likely don't attach — though truthful headers are always required and including them costs nothing. Under GDPR Art. 2(2)(c) and DPDP §3, processing 'in the course of a purely personal or household activity' is out of scope, which a personal job search conducted by the individual plausibly is — but the EXEMPTION covers the job-seeker, NOT a commercial tool/service that processes the data on their behalf (CJEU reads the household exemption narrowly, and the tool vendor is independently a processor/fiduciary). CASL's CEM definition is about encouraging participation in commercial activity; CRTC guidance treats job applications as generally outside it. Practical guidance for the job-seeker: 1:1 personalized messages, real identity, state where you found them, one polite follow-up max, stop on any negative signal, never mass-mail, and prefer addresses confirmed from public sources over blind guesses for Canadian/German recipients.

key_findings.claim: The clean-room design line: accept manual name+company input, derive and verify, store per-user, never touch LinkedIn programmatically
key_findings.detail: Synthesizing all of the above into the SHOULD/SHOULD-NOT boundary — SHOULD: take name+company (or CSV) typed/pasted by the user; look up domain via public DNS/website; rank candidates by the audit priors (first.last 77% → single-token 14% → first.l 6.6%, dot separator) and per-domain KB; verify via MX classification then provider-aware RCPT (skip M365); return confidence-scored results; keep data siloed per user with a retention cap (e.g., 90 days) and one-click delete; maintain a global suppression list honored across all users; log provenance ('derived from user-entered name + public MX') per record to answer access requests Kaspr-style failures cost €240k over. SHOULD NOT: automate any LinkedIn access (no headless browsers, no session-cookie reuse, no 'bring your li_at token' feature, no browser extension that reads LinkedIn DOM — all UA §8.2 violations that also create Kaspr-style GDPR exposure); pool or resell contacts across users (data-broker trigger); bulk-verify entire domains (dictionary-attack pattern); claim 'verified' for M365/catch-all domains (the audit proves it's unknowable — label 'pattern-match, unverifiable'); or auto-send email at all — keep finding and sending as separate, deliberate user acts.

recommendations: Architect the tool as derivation-only: input is user-supplied name+company (manual or CSV), never a LinkedIn URL fetcher, extension, or session-token importer. This single decision removes LinkedIn UA §8.2 breach, hiQ/ProAPIs-style injunction risk, and most of the Kaspr GDPR fact pattern.

Provider-aware verification policy from the audit KB: classify MX first; for *.protection.outlook.com (M365) skip RCPT and return 'pattern-inferred, SMTP-unverifiable' (118 false rejections vs 3 true not-founds); for Google Workspace/Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort allow one RCPT probe per candidate; detect catch-all with a random-local probe and downgrade confidence rather than claim verified.

Hard rate limits as a compliance feature, not just politeness: cap RCPT probes per domain per day (e.g., 10-20), global per-user daily caps, jittered timing, dedicated probe IP separate from any sending IP; refuse 'verify every permutation for a whole company' jobs (CAN-SPAM §7704(b) dictionary-attack pattern).

Build the compliance rails into output, not docs: per-record provenance log (source of name, derivation rule, verification evidence, timestamp); per-user data silo; default retention cap (~90 days) with auto-purge; one-click record deletion; a global cross-user suppression list any recipient can join via a public opt-out page, checked before returning results.

Geo-aware sending guidance surfaced in the UI: US → CAN-SPAM checklist (postal address, opt-out, truthful subject); Canada → warn that permutation-guessed addresses lack CASL implied consent (only conspicuously-published addresses qualify); Germany/Austria → warn B2B cold email needs prior consent (UWG §7); EU generally → generate an Art. 14-compliant first-email disclosure snippet (who/why/source/how-to-object); India → note DPDP substantive obligations from 14 May 2027, consent-only regime for commercial senders.

Ship an LIA (Legitimate Interest Assessment) template pre-filled for the referral-outreach use case using the ICO's three-part test, and store the user's completed LIA with their campaign — this is the artifact an EU regulator asks for first.

For the job-seeker persona specifically: default to 1:1 sends, personalize per recipient, identify yourself and your source truthfully, max one follow-up, honor any 'no' instantly, and never present the tool's output as certainty — show the confidence tier (verified 5.1.1-honest domain > pattern+KB match > blind permutation) so users don't spray guesses that become the #1 bounce cause (address_not_found, 352/3006) and burn their sending domain.

Never pool, share, or resell found contacts across users, and don't build a persistent global people-database — this keeps the tool out of Delete Act data-broker registration, under CCPA thresholds, and out of the Kaspr enforcement template.

Add legal-genuine unsubscribe/opt-out plumbing even where not strictly required (personal job-seeking mail): a mailto/URL opt-out line costs nothing, satisfies CAN-SPAM/CASL if the message is deemed commercial, and materially improves reply rates and deliverability.
caveats: Not legal advice; laws summarized at a US/EU/CA/IN federal level — EU member-state ePrivacy implementations vary widely (Germany, Austria, Italy are opt-in even for B2B) and were not enumerated per-country.
