The 'job-seeker outreach is non-commercial / household-exempt' analysis is a reasonable but untested reading (medium confidence): no court has squarely held that tool-assisted job outreach falls in CAN-SPAM's non-commercial bucket or GDPR's household exemption, and the exemption never covers the tool vendor itself.

Meta v. Bright Data is one district-court summary-judgment ruling (N.D. Cal.), not binding appellate precedent; the logged-off-scraping safe harbor could narrow, and LinkedIn v. ProAPIs settled in principle so it produced no merits ruling.
DPDP Rules 2025 details (14 May 2027 full-compliance date, consent-manager timing) reflect the November 2025 notification as reported by PIB and law-firm summaries; MeitY could amend timelines.
CAN-SPAM per-email penalty ($53,088) is the Jan 2025 inflation adjustment; the FTC adjusts annually so the current 2026 figure may be slightly higher.

SMTP-probing legality is asserted from absence of regulation plus abuse-desk norms, not from any statute or case directly on point; some networks treat any unsolicited RCPT probing as abuse regardless of volume.

Provider-behavior conclusions (M365 useless for RCPT, Google/Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort honest) come from the user's own 3,006-address audit and generalize well but are not universal — individual M365 tenants can enable recipient validation, and some Proofpoint customers front catch-alls.

verification.dimension: Legality & ethics: email finding, LinkedIn data, and cold outreach (GDPR, CAN-SPAM, CASL, DPDP, CCPA, LinkedIn ToS/litigation)

verified_findings.claim: Derived email is still personal data under GDPR/CCPA/DPDP; permutation-derivation itself touches no LinkedIn systems and creates no CFAA/ToS issue — risk concentrates on data-source and sending compliance
verified_findings.evidence: GDPR Art. 4(1) defines personal data as any information relating to an identified/identifiable person; a work email containing a person's name is squarely covered (long-standing WP29/EDPB position, reflected in the CNIL Kaspr decision which treated LinkedIn-derived B2B contact data as personal data). CCPA's B2B exemption lapsed 1 Jan 2023, and India's DPDP Act covers digital personal data with no B2B carve-out. Pure string permutation + DNS/SMTP checks involve no access to LinkedIn systems, so no User Agreement or CFAA hook exists. The architecture split (input provenance + sending compliance carry the risk) is accurate.

verified_findings.claim: hiQ v. LinkedIn ended with hiQ losing: CFAA 'gates-up' ruling for public pages, but Nov 2022 summary judgment for LinkedIn on breach of contract, Dec 2022 consent judgment ($500k, stop scraping, delete data/algorithms)
verified_findings.evidence: 9th Cir. (Apr 2022, post-Van Buren remand) held scraping public pages likely doesn't violate CFAA — correctly framed as 'likely' since it was a preliminary-injunction posture. On 4 Nov 2022, Judge Edward Chen granted LinkedIn summary judgment that hiQ breached the User Agreement (scraping + fake accounts). The 7 Dec 2022 stipulated judgment: $500,000, permanent injunction to cease all scraping and destroy scraped data, source code, and derived algorithms (Proskauer, ZwillGen, Privacy World write-ups). One nuance the finding omits: the $500k stipulation also recited a CFAA violation for hiQ's fake-account access to password-protected pages — stipulated, not adjudicated, so non-precedential. 'hiQ did not bless scraping' is the correct takeaway.

verified_findings.claim: Meta v. Bright Data (N.D. Cal., Jan 2024) protects only logged-off scraping of public pages; LinkedIn v. ProAPIs (Oct 2025, ~1M fake accounts, up to $15k/month) settled in principle in 2026; LinkedIn UA §8.2 bars scraping software including browser plugins
verified_findings.evidence: Judge Edward Chen granted Bright Data summary judgment 23-24 Jan 2024, holding Meta's Terms govern 'your use' of the service and Bright Data's logged-off scraping of public data was not 'use' — expressly narrow, tied to Meta's specific contract language (Quinn Emanuel, Farella client alerts). LinkedIn filed against ProAPIs Inc. and CTO Rehmat Alam in Oct 2025 over >1M fake accounts renting real-time scraped data for up to $15,000/month (BleepingComputer, The Record); Bloomberg Law reports the parties reached an agreement in principle to settle in 2026, terms undisclosed, no merits ruling. LinkedIn's User Agreement 'Dos and Don'ts' (§8.2) prohibits developing, supporting, or using software/scripts/robots/crawlers/browser plugins to scrape — so a vendor automating LinkedIn breaches even if only users hold accounts.

verified_findings.claim: Kaspr fined €240,000 by CNIL (Dec 2024) for scraping LinkedIn contacts including restricted-visibility users, endless 5-year rolling retention, no Art. 14 notice, and vague access-request answers
verified_findings.evidence: CNIL decision of 5 Dec 2024: €240,000 fine (CNIL's own English page 'Data scraping: KASPR fined €240,000'; ~160M contacts via Chrome extension). Violations match: collected details of users who restricted visibility to 1st/2nd-degree connections (no valid legitimate interest); 5-year retention renewed on each job change so data was effectively kept indefinitely; no information to data subjects until 2022 and then English-only. One trap for citations: an EDPB news headline says '€200 000' but links to CNIL's €240,000 release — the CNIL primary source (€240k) controls. CNIL also closed its compliance order against Kaspr in 2025 after remediation. The design lessons drawn (honor visibility settings, retention caps, provenance logging, derivation-only storage) follow directly.

verified_findings.claim: GDPR: B2B cold email can rest on legitimate interest (Recital 47) with documented LIA, Art. 14 notice within 1 month/first communication, absolute Art. 21 opt-out — but ePrivacy national law can forbid it (Germany UWG §7 requires opt-in even B2B)
verified_findings.evidence: Recital 47 final sentence: direct marketing 'may be regarded as carried out for a legitimate interest'. EDPB Guidelines 1/2024 on Art. 6(1)(f) (adopted Oct 2024) require the three-part test; ICO publishes an LIA template. Art. 14(3): notice within a reasonable period, at most one month, or at latest at first communication if data used to communicate with the data subject. Art. 21(2)-(3): objection to direct-marketing processing is unconditional. ePrivacy Directive Art. 13 + German UWG §7(2) treat email advertising without prior express consent as an unreasonable nuisance, with no B2B exception (only the narrow §7(3) existing-customer exemption). Austria and Italy are similarly restrictive, as the finding's caveats note. All accurate.

verified_findings.claim: CAN-SPAM: opt-out regime, no consent needed, B2B included, up to $53,088 per email (Jan 2025 adjustment); Verkada 2024 settlement $2.95M is the largest CAN-SPAM penalty; harvesting/dictionary attacks are aggravated violations under 15 U.S.C. §7704(b)
verified_findings.evidence: FTC compliance guide confirms the formalities (truthful headers, non-deceptive subject, ad identification, physical postal address, opt-out honored within 10 business days) and no B2B exception. $53,088/email confirmed effective 17 Jan 2025; 2026-dated secondary sources still cite $53,088 (I could not confirm a higher 2026 figure — the finding's own caveat stands). DOJ/FTC: Verkada paid $2.95M (settlement announced Aug-Sep 2024), the largest CAN-SPAM penalty to date, over >30M emails including ignored unsubscribes. One sharpening needed: §7704(b) 'aggravated violations' (harvesting, dictionary attacks) attach only in conjunction with initiating messages that violate §7704(a) — RCPT probing alone, with no mail sent, is not itself a CAN-SPAM violation; the finding says 'resembles', which is fair but should be stated precisely.

verified_findings.claim: CASL: opt-in by default; implied consent via 'conspicuously published' address requires publication + no anti-solicitation statement + role-relevance, sender bears burden; a permutation-guessed address cannot qualify; penalties CAD $10M/org, $1M/individual
verified_findings.evidence: CASL s.10(9)(b) and CRTC guidance (crtc.gc.ca/eng/com500/guide.htm) match all three conjunctive conditions, and CRTC enforcement guidance puts the burden of proving consent on the sender, who must keep records. The inference that a never-published, derivation-guessed address fails 'conspicuously published' is logically sound and matches CRTC's reading (the exemption exists because the person chose to publish the address). AMP maxima ($10M corporation / $1M individual per violation) and 10-business-day unsubscribe confirmed. This is a genuine, correctly identified gap for Canadian recipients of guessed addresses.

verified_findings.claim: India DPDP: Rules notified 13 Nov 2025, phased over 18 months with full substantive compliance 14 May 2027, consent-manager registration at 14 Nov 2026; no legitimate-interest ground; penalties to ₹250 crore
verified_findings.evidence: DPDP Rules 2025 notified 13 Nov 2025 (PIB doc, Privacy World, Grant Thornton). Phase II (Rule 4, consent managers) at 12 months and Phase III (Rules 3, 5-16, 22-23) at 18 months confirmed — though most sources state the effective dates as 13 Nov 2026 and 13 May 2027, not the 14th (one-day imprecision). No legitimate-interest basis is correct: DPDP §4 permits only consent or §7 'certain legitimate uses', which do not include cold marketing; §3(c) excludes personal/domestic processing. ₹250 crore is the top Schedule penalty (security-safeguard failures). Material update the finding predates: a Jan 2026 MeitY consultation proposed compressing the 18-month timeline to 12 months — not yet gazetted, but the 14 May 2027 date should be treated as subject to acceleration.
verified_findings.correction: Use 13 Nov 2026 / 13 May 2027 (18 months from the 13 Nov 2025 publication) and add that MeitY's Jan 2026 consultation proposed accelerating full compliance to 12 months (unconfirmed by gazette as of Jul 2026).

verified_findings.claim: CCPA/CPRA: B2B exemption expired 1 Jan 2023; thresholds >$25M revenue OR ≥100k consumers/households OR ≥50% revenue from selling PI; Delete Act (SB 362) makes a contact-selling service a registrable data broker subject to DROP
verified_findings.evidence: B2B/HR exemption sunset on 1 Jan 2023 (IAPP, CPPA FAQ) — correct. Delete Act: data brokers (businesses selling PI of consumers with whom they have no direct relationship) must register with CPPA and honor the centralized DROP mechanism (consumer submissions opened Jan 2026; broker processing obligations began Aug 2026) — the 'never pool/resell contacts' design conclusion is sound. Minor staleness: the revenue threshold is inflation-adjusted and has been $26,625,000 since 1 Jan 2025 (the statute's original $25M figure is what most sources quote; the adjusted figure is the operative one).
verified_findings.correction: State the revenue threshold as ~$26.6M (inflation-adjusted from $25M, effective Jan 2025) rather than a flat $25M.

verified_findings.claim: SMTP RCPT probing: skip M365 entirely (audit: 46 domains, 118 policy 5.4.1 rejections vs 3 true 5.1.1 not-founds), probe once on honest providers (Google Workspace/Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort), keep volumes low, never verify-by-sending
verified_findings.evidence: Verified directly against the grounding data. /Users/akashkumar/linkedin-email-finder/audit_analysis/summary.md: microsoft365 = 46 domains, 810 addrs, 3 addr_not_found vs 118 recipient_rejected (550 5.4.1 fires even for valid mailboxes); google_workspace 175, proofpoint 77, cisco_ironport 52, mimecast 15 honest addr_not_found (550 5.1.1) with near-zero policy rejections. domain_kb.json confirms M365 identification via *.mail.protection.outlook.com MX and per-domain known_bad_locals. Spamhaus has publicly characterized email-verification/list-cleaning probe traffic as an abuse pattern that gets IPs listed — consistent with the claim. The legal framing (no statute directly on point; §7704(b) dictionary-attack analogy only if mail is later sent) is honest. Caveat in the original — individual M365 tenants can enable Recipient Validation, making some tenants honest — is correct and worth keeping.

verified_findings.claim: Individual job-seeker referral outreach: likely non-commercial under CAN-SPAM's primary-purpose test, plausibly within GDPR/DPDP personal-use exclusions, generally not a CEM under CASL — but exemptions never cover the tool vendor
verified_findings.evidence: CAN-SPAM applies to messages whose primary purpose is commercial advertisement/promotion of a product or service (15 U.S.C. §7702(2), FTC primary-purpose rule); an individual inquiry about a job is not advertising a product, though this exact application is untested in court — the medium confidence label is appropriate. GDPR Art. 2(2)(c) household exemption exists but CJEU (Lindqvist, Ryneš) reads it narrowly, and it protects the individual's processing, not a commercial processor acting at scale — the finding gets this key limitation right. CRTC CASL FAQ treats personal relationship messages and job applications as generally outside CEM scope. This is the correctly-hedged, weakest-confidence claim in the set and is labeled as such.

verified_findings.claim: Clean-room design line: manual name+company input, derive+verify with audit priors (first.last 77% → single-token 14% → first.l 6.6%), per-user silo, provenance logging, no LinkedIn automation, no cross-user pooling, no 'verified' label on M365/catch-all
verified_findings.evidence: Priors match grounding data exactly: 2313/3006 = 76.9% first.last (summary.md table rounds to 76%), 421/3006 = 14.0% single_token, 200/3006 = 6.65% first.l, dot separator 2535/3006. 'M365 unverifiable' is proven by the 118-vs-3 ratio. Each SHOULD-NOT maps to a verified legal anchor: LinkedIn automation → UA §8.2 + hiQ/ProAPIs; pooling/resale → Delete Act broker registration + Kaspr fact pattern; bulk domain verification → §7704(b) dictionary-attack pattern (aggravated only if mail is sent, but independently an abuse-desk/blocklist risk); provenance logging → the exact Art. 15 failure CNIL cited against Kaspr. Sound synthesis, no errors found.

must_fix_errors: DPDP effective dates: use 13 Nov 2026 (consent-manager registration) and 13 May 2027 (substantive obligations) — 18 months from the 13 Nov 2025 publication; the findings' '14 May 2027 / 14 Nov 2026' is off by one day. Also add: MeitY's Jan 2026 stakeholder consultation proposed accelerating full compliance from 18 to 12 months (not yet gazetted as of Jul 2026), so the 2027 date may move earlier — treat it as a floor, not a promise.

CAN-SPAM §7704(b) framing: aggravated violations (address harvesting, dictionary attacks) attach only IN CONJUNCTION with initiating email that violates §7704(a). RCPT probing with no mail sent is not a CAN-SPAM violation at all; the risk of bulk permutation-probing is abuse-desk/blocklist and future-sending aggravation, not standalone liability. Keep the rate limits, fix the legal characterization.
CCPA applicability threshold: the revenue prong is inflation-adjusted — $26,625,000 effective 1 Jan 2025, not a flat $25M.

research.dimension: Reference architectures & open-source prior art for email permutation + verification

key_findings.claim: The proven design splits into three decoupled stages: candidate generation -> verification pipeline -> scoring/classification. Every serious tool (Reacher, Hunter, deep-email-validator) follows this shape.
key_findings.detail: Stage 1 Candidate generation: take {first, middle, last, domain} and emit ranked permutations (first.last, flast, first, first_last, f.last, firstl, etc.). Simple libs (Satys/python-email-permutator, jacobgoh101/email-permutator, hippiiee/MailPermute) do only this and just enumerate combinatorially. Our audit lets us do far better than blind enumeration: rank first.last first (76%), single_token (14%), first.l (6.6%), separator=dot by default, and when domain_kb.json has a learned pattern for the domain, generate ONLY that pattern. Stage 2 Verification: layered, cheapest-first — syntax -> disposable/role/typo -> MX/DNS -> SMTP RCPT probe -> catch-all probe. Reacher's module split (syntax/mx/smtp/misc) is the canonical decomposition. Stage 3 Scoring: fold the deterministic checks plus the audit's pattern-prior into a single confidence score and a status label (valid/invalid/accept-all/risky/unknown), exactly Hunter's model.

key_findings.claim: Recommended Python stack: dnspython (MX), email-validator by JoshData (syntax + normalization), aiosmtplib (async SMTP probing), plus a small hand-written permutation module and a disposable/role-account list. Licensing is permissive across the board.
key_findings.detail: dnspython — MX/A record lookup, mature, ISC license (permissive). email-validator (JoshData/python-email-validator) — RFC-aware syntax validation, Unicode/IDNA normalization, optional MX deliverability check; license is the Unlicense / public domain (CC0 before Feb 2024), so zero restrictions — use it for syntax+normalization but DISABLE its network deliverability check and run your own. aiosmtplib — async SMTP client, MIT license, lets you run hundreds of RCPT probes concurrently with per-connection timeouts (essential for throughput). The old 'validate_email' (syrusakbary) is syntax-only/regex and largely unmaintained/LGPL — skip it in favor of email-validator. For disposable/role detection, vendor a static JSON list (e.g. the disposable-email-domains list, CC0/MIT) rather than a live API. Write the permutation generator yourself (~50 lines) so ranking is driven by your audit priors — don't take a dependency for it.

key_findings.claim: Reacher (check-if-email-exists) is the best reference architecture to STUDY but is AGPL-3.0 (dual-licensed commercial) — read it for design, do not vendor or link it into a proprietary product.
key_findings.detail: Reacher outputs a top-level is_reachable in {safe, risky, invalid, unknown} composed from sub-objects: syntax.is_valid_syntax, mx.accepts_mail + mx.records, smtp.can_connect_smtp / is_deliverable / is_catch_all / is_disabled / has_full_inbox, and misc.is_disposable / is_role_account. Key lessons to copy: (1) it special-cases big providers with a VerifyMethod enum {Smtp, Api, Headless} because plain RCPT fails on them — Gmail/Yahoo/Hotmail get API or headless-login checks instead of SMTP; (2) it uses ~45s connection timeout with NO retry to reduce greylisting-induced false unknowns; (3) SOCKS5 proxy support is built in because the connecting IP matters. The AGPL means any network use of their code obligates source disclosure — so treat it as a spec, not a dependency. deep-email-validator (Node, MIT) is a cleaner license if a JS reference is wanted; it does regex+typo(mailcheck)+disposable+MX+SMTP over port 25.

key_findings.claim: PITFALL 1 — Port 25 is blocked on virtually all cloud/residential networks, and connecting from a listed IP corrupts results, not just throughput.
key_findings.detail: AWS, GCP, Azure, Railway and most residential ISPs block outbound TCP/25. Separately, Spamhaus PBL lists dynamic/residential/cloud ranges as 'must not talk direct-to-MX'; probing from a PBL/blocklisted IP means target MX servers may drop, tarpit, or 550 you regardless of whether the mailbox exists — producing false negatives. Mitigation: run the SMTP-probe worker on a dedicated VPS/bare-metal with a clean static IP and PTR/rDNS set, with port 25 unblocked (request unblock from provider); use a pool of such IPs behind SOCKS5 and rotate; keep probe volume per target domain low to avoid rate-based blocks; and always have a graceful fallback to 'unknown' (never 'invalid') when the connection itself fails.

key_findings.claim: PITFALL 2 — False confidence from catch-all and Microsoft 365, directly corroborated by our own bounce audit. RCPT probing is only trustworthy on honest providers.
key_findings.detail: Catch-all: many B2B domains 250-accept every RCPT. Standard detection = probe a random gibberish local-part at the domain first; if it 250s, the domain is accept-all and any specific-address 250 is meaningless -> label 'accept-all/risky', fall back to pattern-prior confidence, never 'valid'. Provider gate (our audit ground truth): Microsoft 365 (mx *.protection.outlook.com) returned 550 5.4.1 'Access denied' as a policy block for 118 valid-ish addresses and only 3 true 550 5.1.1 not-founds — RCPT probing is USELESS on M365, so DETECT M365 from MX and skip the probe, scoring purely on pattern prior. Conversely Google Workspace, Proofpoint, Mimecast, Cisco IronPort returned honest 550 5.1.1 address_not_found -> RCPT probing WORKS and should be weighted heavily there. Gmail/Yahoo consumer MX also anti-harvest (accept-all-ish) — use provider-specific handling like Reacher, not raw RCPT. So the pipeline MUST branch on provider (from MX) before deciding whether an SMTP result is even meaningful.

key_findings.claim: PITFALL 3 — Greylisting and transient codes must map to 'unknown' + retry, never to 'invalid'; and probes must be async with strict timeouts or the tool is unusably slow.
key_findings.detail: A 450/451 (or connection timeout) is a temporary defer, commonly greylisting where the server rejects an unknown connecting IP the first time. Treating it as invalid produces false rejects of real mailboxes. Correct handling: classify 4xx/timeout as 'unknown' and retry after a delay (Reacher instead uses one long ~45s timeout with no retry to dodge greylisting entirely). SMTP probes take 2-5s each, so run them with aiosmtplib concurrently (bounded semaphore), cache MX + catch-all status + learned pattern per domain (this is what domain_kb.json is for), and dedupe by domain so you probe the catch-all/provider fingerprint once, not per address. Also reuse a single MAIL FROM/EHLO session to batch multiple RCPTs to the same MX where the server allows it.

key_findings.claim: Scoring model to adopt: a 4-label status (valid / invalid / accept-all / unknown) plus a 0-100 confidence, fusing deterministic SMTP evidence with the audit-derived pattern prior — this is exactly how Hunter separates 'deliverability status' from 'confidence score'.
key_findings.detail: Hunter returns status in {valid, invalid, accept-all, unknown} AND a confidence score, and explicitly notes the score matters MOST for accept-all domains where SMTP can't confirm. Concretely for our tool: (a) syntax/MX fail -> invalid, conf 0; (b) honest-provider RCPT 550 5.1.1 -> invalid, conf ~0; (c) honest-provider RCPT 250 (non-catch-all) -> valid, conf 90-98; (d) catch-all or M365 or Gmail/Yahoo -> status accept-all/unknown, confidence = f(pattern prior): if the guess matches domain_kb.json's learned dominant shape use 75-90, if it's just the global 76% first.last prior use ~55-65, unusual shape lower; (e) role account (info@/sales@) or disposable domain -> risky flag regardless. Persist every observed (domain, provider, shape, result) back into domain_kb.json so confidence self-improves per domain over time — the single highest-leverage design choice, and one none of the simple OSS permutators do.

recommendations: Python stack: dnspython (MX, ISC) + email-validator/JoshData (syntax+normalization, Unlicense — but disable its built-in deliverability check) + aiosmtplib (async SMTP probing, MIT) + a hand-written audit-driven permutation ranker + a vendored disposable/role JSON list. All permissive; avoids any copyleft.

Study Reacher/check-if-email-exists as the architectural spec (module split: syntax/mx/smtp/misc; is_reachable={safe,risky,invalid,unknown}; VerifyMethod enum for big providers; 45s no-retry timeout; SOCKS5 proxy) but do NOT vendor or link it — it is AGPL-3.0.

Structure the code as three decoupled stages with clean interfaces: generate_candidates(name, domain) -> [ranked guesses]; verify(email) -> evidence dict; score(evidence, pattern_prior) -> (status, confidence). Keep verification cheapest-first (syntax -> role/disposable -> MX -> SMTP -> catch-all).

Branch the verification pipeline on provider fingerprinted from MX BEFORE trusting any SMTP code: M365 (*.protection.outlook.com) and Gmail/Yahoo consumer -> skip/replace raw RCPT, score on pattern prior; Google Workspace/Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort -> trust honest 550 5.1.1. This is the direct payoff of the bounce audit.

Always run a random-local-part catch-all probe per domain first; on 250 mark the domain accept-all and downgrade every guess there to risky/confidence-only. Cache MX, provider, catch-all status, and learned pattern per domain (extend domain_kb.json) and probe each domain's fingerprint once, not per address.

Run SMTP probing on a dedicated clean-IP VPS with port 25 unblocked and correct PTR/rDNS, ideally an IP pool behind SOCKS5; never probe from cloud/residential IPs (port 25 blocked + Spamhaus PBL corrupts results). Map all 4xx/timeouts/connection failures to 'unknown', never 'invalid'.

Adopt Hunter's dual output: a 4-value status (valid/invalid/accept-all/unknown) plus a 0-100 confidence, and feed the audit pattern priors (first.last 76%, single-token 14%, first.l 6.6%, dot separator) into confidence when SMTP is inconclusive. Write results back to the KB so per-domain confidence self-improves.

Include misc checks cheaply: disposable-domain list, role-account regex (info|sales|support|admin|hr|careers), and typo/mailcheck-style suggestion — these are standard in deep-email-validator and Reacher and prevent obviously-bad guesses from consuming SMTP budget.
caveats: Licenses were confirmed from project pages for dnspython (ISC), aiosmtplib (MIT), email-validator (Unlicense/CC0), Reacher (AGPL-3.0), and deep-email-validator (MIT). The legacy 'validate_email' (syrusakbary) and various 'verify-email' packages have inconsistent/older licenses (LGPL and others) and weak maintenance — I recommend against them rather than having pinned exact terms; verify before use if you reconsider.

Simple OSS permutators (Satys, jacobgoh101, hippiiee/MailPermute, syedahmedz) are candidate-generation only and mostly do naive combinatorial enumeration or rely on defunct services (Rapportive) for verification — useful as pattern references, not as dependencies. Their star/maintenance quality is generally low.

Provider behaviors evolve: Yahoo historically accept-all but many MX now reject honestly; M365 policy codes can change. The provider-gating rules should be data-driven from your own KB (re-derived over time), not hard-coded permanently.

SMTP verification of Gmail/Outlook consumer and M365 corporate mailboxes is fundamentally unreliable by design (anti-harvesting/policy) — no library solves this; the only robust signal there is the pattern prior plus (optionally) provider-specific API/headless methods, which carry their own ToS and fragility risks.

I did not benchmark throughput/accuracy of these libraries empirically; recommendations are based on documented design, licensing, and your bounce-audit ground truth, not on running them against your list.

verification.dimension: Reference architectures & open-source prior art for email permutation + verification

verified_findings.claim: Reacher (check-if-email-exists) is AGPL-3.0, dual-licensed with a commercial license; study it as a spec, do not vendor/link into proprietary code.
verified_findings.evidence: Reacher README/docs state a dual-license model: Commercial License for commercial use, AGPL-3.0 for open-source use compatible with AGPLv3. AGPL's network-use copyleft is correctly described. (github.com/reacherhq/check-if-email-exists README; help.reacher.email/faq)

verified_findings.claim: Reacher's top-level is_reachable = {safe, risky, invalid, unknown}, composed from syntax/mx/smtp/misc sub-objects.
verified_findings.evidence: docs.reacher.email 'Understanding is_reachable' documents exactly {safe, risky, invalid, unknown} with the stated semantics (unknown = port 25 blocked / IP blacklisted / provider disallows realtime verification). Module split syntax/mx/smtp/misc matches the crate.

verified_findings.claim: Reacher special-cases big providers with a VerifyMethod enum {Smtp, Api, Headless}; Gmail/Yahoo/Hotmail get API or headless instead of plain RCPT.
verified_findings.evidence: CLI exposes --gmail-verif-method (api|smtp, default smtp), --yahoo-verif-method (api|headless|smtp, default headless), and Hotmail B2B=Smtp, B2C=Smtp|Headless; headless needs RCH_WEBDRIVER_ADDR. Enum naming is essentially correct.

verified_findings.claim: Reacher uses ~45s connection timeout with NO retry to dodge greylisting.
verified_findings.evidence: Reacher does expose a configurable SMTP timeout and does map transient failures to 'unknown', but I could not confirm the specific '45s, no retry' default from README/docs. SMTP timeout is configurable, not a fixed 45s.
verified_findings.correction: State the timeout is configurable and that transient/timeout -> unknown; drop or hedge the exact '45s no-retry' figure unless confirmed from source.

verified_findings.claim: Python stack licenses: dnspython=ISC, email-validator (JoshData)=Unlicense (CC0 before Feb 2024), aiosmtplib=MIT, deep-email-validator=MIT — all permissive, no copyleft.
verified_findings.evidence: dnspython LICENSE = ISC (dnspython.org/LICENSE). JoshData/python-email-validator switched to the Unlicense; CC0 1.0 prior to 2024-02-04 (repo CHANGELOG/LICENSE). aiosmtplib = MIT (PyPI). mfbx9da4/deep-email-validator = MIT (repo LICENSE).

verified_findings.claim: Advice to use email-validator for syntax/normalization but DISABLE its built-in network deliverability check and run your own.
verified_findings.evidence: email-validator's validate_email() has a check_deliverability flag (default True) that does a DNS/MX lookup; disabling it and running your own MX+SMTP pipeline is correct and standard.

verified_findings.claim: Hunter separates a status {valid, invalid, accept-all, unknown} from a 0-100 confidence score, and the score matters most for accept-all where SMTP can't confirm.
verified_findings.evidence: Hunter Help Center: statuses valid/accept-all/invalid/unknown (plus disposable), and Confidence Score 'most useful for Accept-All emails'; ~85-95%+ treated as likely deliverable. Note Hunter's full label set also includes disposable/webmail flags, so '4-label' is a slight simplification but the model is accurate.

verified_findings.claim: Port 25 is blocked on virtually all cloud/residential networks; Spamhaus PBL lists cloud/residential ranges as 'must not talk direct-to-MX', so probing from such IPs corrupts results (false negatives), not just throughput.
verified_findings.evidence: Spamhaus PBL FAQ: PBL lists IP space that should not send email directly to MX (broadband/dynamic/end-user, incl. cloud), covers ~1.4B IPv4; AWS/GCP/Azure block outbound 25 by default requiring approval. Mitigations (clean static IP + PTR, IP pool, low per-domain volume, fallback to 'unknown') are sound.

verified_findings.claim: Audit ground truth: M365 (*.protection.outlook.com) returned 550 5.4.1 policy blocks for 118 addresses and only 3 true 550 5.1.1 not-founds -> RCPT probing useless on M365; Google Workspace/Proofpoint/Mimecast/IronPort returned honest 550 5.1.1 -> RCPT works there.
verified_findings.evidence: Reconciled against summary.md provider table: microsoft365 recipient_rejected=118, address_not_found=3; google_workspace addr_not_found=175, proofpoint=77, cisco_ironport=52, mimecast=15 with recip_rejected~0. Provider-gating from MX before trusting SMTP codes is directly supported by the data.

verified_findings.claim: Three-stage decoupled design (candidate generation -> layered cheapest-first verification -> scoring), with catch-all detection via random-local-part probe, is the canonical shape used by Reacher/Hunter/deep-email-validator.
verified_findings.evidence: Matches Reacher module split and deep-email-validator (regex+typo/mailcheck+disposable+dns+smtp over port 25, MIT). Random-gibberish catch-all probe -> downgrade to accept-all/risky is standard practice; consistent with Hunter's accept-all handling.

verified_findings.claim: Legacy 'validate_email' (syrusakbary) is syntax-only/regex, LGPL, and largely unmaintained.
verified_findings.evidence: validate_email by Syrus Akbary is LGPL and unmaintained (correct), but it is NOT syntax-only: it offers check_mx and verify (SMTP RCPT) options. Calling it 'syntax-only/regex' is inaccurate.
verified_findings.correction: Describe it as an old, unmaintained LGPL library that also did MX/SMTP checks (not merely regex); recommend against it for maintenance/license reasons, not because it lacks verification.

must_fix_errors: Finding #2 caveat mischaracterizes syrusakbary/validate_email as 'syntax-only/regex' — it also performs MX and SMTP verification. Fix the description (still fine to recommend against it for being unmaintained/LGPL).
Drop or explicitly hedge the unverified 'Reacher uses ~45s timeout with NO retry' specific — Reacher's SMTP timeout is configurable; only the transient->unknown behavior is confirmable.
